Aisha Bello: Overnight we were closer to ~40–70 on some models because QPS caps were conservative.

Aisha Bello: Suggest:
- paging: min_samples 300 over 30m (or 200 over 45m)
- warning: keep 120 over 15m

Sean Gallagher: +1. For pages we can afford slower detection; for warnings we want earlier signal.

Logan Wright: Caution: expanding models/regions will make meeting 300 samples harder unless we increase canary budget.

Noah Patel: We can also do “min_samples OR min_runtime_seconds” (e.g., require >=30m runtime) so we don’t page on a spiky 10m burst.

Allison Grant: Anti-flap: I’d like a “for:” clause that’s longer for paging.

Allison Grant: Right now paging has `for: 15m`. We could do `for: 30m` and require two consecutive evaluation intervals above threshold.

Rafael Mendes: That’s fine. The real incident we care about is “sustained regression” anyway.

Vanessa Ortiz: Also can we add a hard suppression during known fleet events? We already emit `fleet_change_event` in internal status; could be used to annotate dashboards at least.

Aisha Bello: Annotation is easy. Auto-suppress based on fleet events might be risky (we might miss real regressions coinciding with fleet work). I’d rather annotate + include “recent fleet change” in alert payload.

Rafael Mendes: Agree: annotate + payload context.

incident-bot: (note) supported context fields: release_id, runtime_build, kernel_id, region, hw_sku, model, baseline_id, dashboard_url, runbook_url

Allison Grant: Dedupe proposal (action): incident-bot v4
- dedupe_key = `alertname|severity|model|region`
- thread title text includes model/region/severity (so it’s obvious)
- subsequent alerts with different hw_sku/runtime_build get appended as replies in same thread

Rafael Mendes: Please do. Also: if we’re already in “paging incident” thread, can incident-bot suppress subsequent pages and just comment?

Allison Grant: Yes, we can add a “cooldown” per dedupe_key for PagerDuty events. Something like: once a page is sent, don’t send another PD event for 2h unless severity increases (Sev2->Sev1).

Sean Gallagher: That’s what we want. Cooldown + escalation only.

Nadia Rahman: On baseline drift: I can add a “baseline_age_hours” field and we can gate paging if baseline is older than X hours AND runtime_build changed recently.

Noah Patel: +1. Also add explicit “baseline_version != current_runtime_build” flag in the canary payload.

Aisha Bello: Summarizing proposed tuning (tell me if I missed anything):
1) Paging min_samples up + longer `for:`
2) Dedupe key fix + PD cooldown
3) Baseline staleness suppression for paging for N hours after runtime patch deploy
4) Make paging SKU-aware (>=2 SKUs or dominant SKU) to avoid mix-shift noise
5) Dashboard annotations for fleet events; include context in alert

Rafael Mendes: Looks right.

Sean Gallagher: Add one more: tighten what actually qualifies for paging. Week 1 we paged at +10% p95 for top models. That might be too aggressive given variance.

Sean Gallagher: Maybe page at +15% sustained for top-3 models, +20% for rest of top-10, and everything else warning only.

Aisha Bello: Works for me. We can encode “top_model=true” in config and have the alert rule use different thresholds.

Noah Patel: We already have top-model list in control plane config for canary targets. Can export that as a label for alert routing? (careful on cardinality)

Logan Wright: A boolean label `top_model` is fine. Avoid adding “rank=1..10” etc.

Allison Grant: Great. I’ll draft the PRs:
- incident-bot: dedupe + cooldown + thread append
- observability-pack: adjust alert rules (min_samples, for:, thresholds)

Rafael Mendes: Please link here when up. I’ll update the oncall doc + tell rotation we’re tuning and to expect fewer pages next week.

Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll add a doc snippet on expected fleet event annotations + where to check recent changes.

Aisha Bello: I’ll adjust the Grafana panels too so the “what changed?” links are more prominent (runtime build / kernel id / fleet).

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. Goal: by Monday we can expand without torching the rotation.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: will post PRs shortly.

source.thread:
Aisha Bello: Heads up on perf-canary alerts: links are a bit of a mess right now. Some point to us-east Grafana even when the alert is from eu-west. Also trace links sometimes 404 (wrong env). Can we fix before enabling paging in phase 1?

Rafael Mendes: +1. On-call can’t spend 5 mins rebuilding URLs in the middle of a page. We need “click -> exact panel + exact model + correct region”.

Noah Patel: That’s on me / alert payload template. Right now we’re emitting `dashboard_url` from a hardcoded base.

Allison Grant: What’s the desired set of links? My vote: 1) dashboard panel (per-model overview) 2) “breakdown” dashboard (prefill/decode/queue) 3) trace search link scoped to canary service and model 4) runbook.

Aisha Bello: yes. For traces, we should link to the pre-built query with `service=perf-canary` and tags (model, region, runtime_build, kernel_id if present). Even if it returns “no results” it’s better than landing in the generic APM home.

Logan Wright: Regional routing: Grafana is deployed per region for perf reasons. The safe rule: dashboard links should be relative to the region the metric came from (same telemetry cluster). We can map `region -> grafana_base_url` via config.

Caleb Johnson: For traces: the UI base is also region-specific. We *can* build a link to the “Search” page with query params, but the params differ slightly between prod/staging. We should keep it simple:
- if `trace_id` is available, link direct to trace
- otherwise link to search with minimal filters (service + time range)

Nadia Rahman: Are we actually capturing trace_id in the alert path? The evaluator runs off metrics, so we might not have a trace_id.

Noah Patel: We don’t have trace_id today. We can add a “recent sample” fetch but that’s bigger. Maybe for MVP the trace link should be a search query over last 30m.

Allison Grant: Agree. MVP: deterministic search link, no promise of a specific trace. But it must not 404.

Rafael Mendes: Also needs to work for dedicated pools. Some alerts are `tier=dedicated` and the dashboards differ (dedicated has additional panels). If we can’t detect, at least land in the top-level perf-canary dashboard with variables set.

Aisha Bello: Can we standardize on a single dashboard UID with vars? e.g.
- dashboard: Perf Canary / Per-model Overview
- vars: `model`, `region`, `tier`, `hardware_sku`, `runtime_build`
And then Grafana handles filtering.

Logan Wright: Yep, that’s the intended design. UIDs are global *within* a region’s Grafana, but not across regions. So we still need `grafana_base_url` correct.

Caleb Johnson: Trace UI base URL mapping can follow same pattern. Something like:
`TRACING_BASE_URL_BY_REGION[region]`
Then link:
`/search?service=perf-canary&from=now-30m&to=now&tags=model:<model>,region:<region>`
(Exact params TBD, but we can keep it stable.)

Connor O'Brien: From release-gates POV: alert payload needs to include links that our bot can render and that are stable. If we change link schema, tell me so I can update gate parser.

Noah Patel: Proposed payload fields (strings) to keep stable:
- `links.dashboard_overview`
- `links.dashboard_breakdown`
- `links.traces`
- `links.runbook`
- `links.baseline_artifact` (optional)
But… incident-bot handler currently expects `dashboard_url` and `runbook_url`. We can add new ones without breaking.

incident-bot: (auto) FYI current handler renders: dashboard_url, runbook_url, and a "details" block if present.

Allison Grant: Please avoid nested JSON in the webhook payload if we can (Slack formatting is easier). But multiple links are ok if we render as bullets.

Rafael Mendes: +1. In Slack, give me something like:
- Dashboard (overview)
- Dashboard (breakdown)
- Traces (search)
- Runbook
No hidden fields.

Nadia Rahman: Can we also include the exact dashboard vars used, so if a link breaks we can still copy/paste values into Grafana? Like `model=... region=...`.

Aisha Bello: Yes. But put it in the text block, not another link.

Noah Patel: Action item: I’ll patch perf-canary alert formatter to:
1) select `grafana_base_url` and `tracing_base_url` from config based on `region`
2) generate dashboard URLs using UID + vars (URL-encoded)
3) include a traces search link last 30m
4) include runbook link to `runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert`

Logan Wright: Please also handle the case where `region` is missing (shouldn’t happen, but). Default to `us-east` and add `link_warning=missing_region` in the message body.

Caleb Johnson: I can send you the current trace search URL format for prod. There was a recent change in query param name from `tags` to `attributes` in the UI.

Noah Patel: Great, DM me. I’ll keep it behind a helper so we can update without touching alert rules.

Allison Grant: Before enabling paging: can we test links end-to-end? Like generate a fake alert for eu-west and click through.

Aisha Bello: I’ll do a quick checklist:
- us-east hosted
- us-east dedicated
- eu-west hosted
And confirm all open correctly and variables are populated.

Rafael Mendes: Also: make sure incident-bot dedupe key doesn’t include the full URL (we had that issue once… dedupe exploded because URL had time range). If trace link has `now-30m`, it’s stable enough, but don’t include absolute timestamps.

Noah Patel: Good call. I’ll keep the trace link relative (now-30m/now) and ensure dedupe key is `(model, region, tier, metric_type)`.

Connor O'Brien: Once done, drop an example payload in #eng-releases so I can validate gate parsing.

Aisha Bello: Thanks all. Please aim to land today/tomorrow; we’re close to flipping warning->paging in us-east.

Noah Patel: ETA: PR up EOD. Will tag @Aisha Bello @Rafael Mendes for link click-test.

source.messages:
Aisha Bello: we should draft the statuspage comms playbook now, before we flip more traffic to tiered batching. what are the criteria for posting if latency worsens during rollout?

Rafael Mendes: +1. we’ve got abort criteria for canary, but statuspage criteria is separate (customer-visible). don’t want to over-post for a 1% canary blip.

Sean Gallagher: agreed. let’s define:
1) when we post
2) what we say (don’t overspec internal feature names)
3) who owns updates + cadence

Allison Grant: from metrics side, suggest we anchor on customer-visible SLOs and impact scope.
- Hosted API: fast tier p99 (streaming first-token) + standard tier p95 end-to-end are the ones folks feel
- also “time to first token” for streaming routes is super sensitive

Marissa Cole: also need a line on “if this is limited to canary cohort” -> we can do targeted comms (support + affected customers) instead of global statuspage.

Aisha Bello: ok propose triggers (hosted):
A) p99 TTFT for streaming routes in FAST tier > target by +25% for >= 15 min AND >5% traffic impacted
B) p95 end-to-end latency in STANDARD tier > target by +20% for >= 30 min AND >10% traffic impacted
C) any p99 > target by +50% for >= 10 min in 2+ regions
D) if we shed load / throttle (customer gets 429/503) due to overload mode as part of mitigation

Julian Moore: + include queueing signals? if we see queue depth runaway we’ll rollback fast, but that’s still “degraded performance” while happening.

Sean Gallagher: queue depth is internal; use it as investigation hint, not a public trigger. public triggers should be measurable externally.

Rafael Mendes: on traffic impacted: how do we compute? % of requests in component? e.g. /v1/chat completions only? or all inference?

Allison Grant: we can compute impact at two levels:
- request volume weighted across Hosted API inference endpoints
- additionally list “subset of endpoints” in message if only chat is affected
We already tag by route + model, but for statuspage keep it simple.

Aisha Bello: +1. impact scope on statuspage should read like “some customers in us-east-1 may see elevated latency for streaming responses”

Connor O'Brien: release train angle: during staged rollout we may have “intentional” changes (like cost-efficient tier enabling). still, if it degrades beyond SLO we should treat as incident.

Sean Gallagher: yes. rollout isn’t a free pass. criteria should be “customer experience degradation beyond published targets and not quickly self-resolving.”

Mateo Alvarez: (jumping in) strongly prefer we post if user-visible and persistent, even if we expect rollback soon. but avoid saying “new batching defaults broke x” publicly.

Marissa Cole: wording guidance:
- don’t mention “tiered batching” or internal flags
- do mention “we’re investigating elevated inference latency”
- if rollback executed: “we’ve reverted a recent change”
- include ETA only if real; otherwise “next update in 30 min”

Allison Grant: also define “post vs no-post” for canary:
- If canary is <2% traffic and ONLY affects canary cohort AND we can confirm isolation, no statuspage; notify support + internal #incidents and fix/rollback.
- If canary cohort includes paying customers broadly (not isolated) OR impact bleeds into non-canary, treat as normal incident -> statuspage.

Rafael Mendes: we should codify “isolation confirmation” requirements:
1) feature flag scope confirmed (flag service screenshot / config snapshot)
2) metrics show impacted requests have `tiered_defaults_enabled=true`
3) no correlated spike on non-enabled cohort

Aisha Bello: good. let’s also add “customer tickets” trigger: if Support gets 3+ independent reports in 20 min (same symptom/region), we post even if metrics are borderline.

SupportBot: FYI Support intake rule updated: tag “latency-regression” auto-escalates to #eng-sre-oncall when count >= 3 within 30m.

Sean Gallagher: nice. for thresholds, I’d tweak:
- FAST streaming TTFT p99: if > +30% for >= 10 min (fast tier is supposed to be strict)
- STANDARD p95: +20% for >= 30 min is ok
- COST-EFFICIENT: don’t page/statuspage based solely on that tier unless it impacts default traffic (since not default early)

Julian Moore: watch out: cost-efficient could become default later. playbook should be “current default tier(s) + any tier impacting >10% traffic.”

Allison Grant: can we add an explicit “error budget burn rate” trigger? e.g. 14-day SLO burn > 2x for 30 min.

Aisha Bello: for statuspage, burn rate is helpful internally but might delay posting. I’d keep as internal gate for paging severity.

Sean Gallagher: agree. statuspage trigger should be direct latency + scope + duration.

Rafael Mendes: what about partial region? we have region components on statuspage?

Marissa Cole: currently statuspage has “Hosted API” + “Console” + “Dedicated Control Plane” components. Not per-region. We can still mention region(s) in the body.

Connor O'Brien: update cadence:
- initial “Investigating” within 10 min of decision
- next update every 30 min until mitigated
- postmortem note not on statuspage, but we can link to incident summary if customer asks

Sean Gallagher: owner + approver:
- oncall SRE (me / primary) drafts + posts
- release manager (Rafael / Connor) provides context “rollback in progress”
- PM on-call (Marissa / Nikhil) sanity-check language if available, but don’t block if urgent

Aisha Bello: template v0:
1) Investigating: “We’re investigating elevated inference latency affecting some requests to the Hosted API. This may impact time-to-first-token and overall response times. Next update in 30 minutes.”
2) Identified/Mitigating: “We identified the cause and are rolling back a recent change / adjusting capacity. We’re monitoring recovery.”
3) Monitoring: “Latency is returning to normal levels. We’ll continue monitoring.”
4) Resolved: “This incident has been resolved. If you continue seeing issues, contact support.”

Allison Grant: add specific: mention 429/503 only if true. otherwise keep to latency.

Julian Moore: and avoid promising exact recovery time unless we’ve verified rollback ETA.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. also: if the issue is isolated to streaming endpoints, say that explicitly (customers doing non-streaming won’t panic).

Rafael Mendes: decision tree proposal:
- If impact >=10% traffic OR multiple customer reports OR 2+ regions AND duration >=10-15m -> statuspage
- Else if strictly canary + isolated -> no statuspage, internal + support-only
- If we trigger load shedding -> statuspage immediately

Sean Gallagher: sounds right. Aisha can you write this into the rollout checklist doc? I’ll add to oncall runbook as “Statuspage criteria - tiered defaults rollout.”

Aisha Bello: yep. I’ll post the criteria + templates in the rollout checklist and link here. also adding a checkbox: “statuspage decision made (Y/N) + rationale” for canary steps.

Marissa Cole: last note: for any statuspage post during rollout, also post a short internal blurb in #support and #customer-success so they can handle inbound.

Connor O'Brien: + we should pre-create the incident in statuspage for fast posting? (draft saved)

Sean Gallagher: do it. but keep it unpublished until needed. ok shipping this playbook.

Aisha Bello: cool, I’ll update with final thresholds:
- FAST streaming TTFT p99 > +30% for 10m AND >5% traffic
- STANDARD e2e p95 > +20% for 30m AND >10% traffic
- ANY tier p99 > +50% for 10m in 2+ regions OR load shedding -> immediate
- 3+ support reports in 20m -> immediate review for posting

Allison Grant: works. I’ll ensure dashboards have a “traffic impacted” panel to make this fast.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the release brief too so it’s not tribal knowledge.

- COST-EFFICIENT: don’t page/statuspage based solely on that tier unless it impacts default traffic (since not default early)

Julian Moore: watch out: cost-efficient could become default later. playbook should be “current default tier(s) + any tier impacting >10% traffic.”

Allison Grant: can we add an explicit “error budget burn rate” trigger? e.g. 14-day SLO burn > 2x for 30 min.

Aisha Bello: for statuspage, burn rate is helpful internally but might delay posting. I’d keep as internal gate for paging severity.

Sean Gallagher: agree. statuspage trigger should be direct latency + scope + duration.

Rafael Mendes: what about partial region? we have region components on statuspage?

Marissa Cole: currently statuspage has “Hosted API” + “Console” + “Dedicated Control Plane” components. Not per-region. We can still mention region(s) in the body.

Connor O'Brien: update cadence:
- initial “Investigating” within 10 min of decision
- next update every 30 min until mitigated
- postmortem note not on statuspage, but we can link to incident summary if customer asks

Sean Gallagher: owner + approver:
- oncall SRE (me / primary) drafts + posts
- release manager (Rafael / Connor) provides context “rollback in progress”
- PM on-call (Marissa / Nikhil) sanity-check language if available, but don’t block if urgent

Aisha Bello: template v0:
1) Investigating: “We’re investigating elevated inference latency affecting some requests to the Hosted API. This may impact time-to-first-token and overall response times. Next update in 30 minutes.”
2) Identified/Mitigating: “We identified the cause and are rolling back a recent change / adjusting capacity. We’re monitoring recovery.”
3) Monitoring: “Latency is returning to normal levels. We’ll continue monitoring.”
4) Resolved: “This incident has been resolved. If you continue seeing issues, contact support.”

Allison Grant: add specific: mention 429/503 only if true. otherwise keep to latency.

Julian Moore: and avoid promising exact recovery time unless we’ve verified rollback ETA.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. also: if the issue is isolated to streaming endpoints, say that explicitly (customers doing non-streaming won’t panic).

Rafael Mendes: decision tree proposal:
- If impact >=10% traffic OR multiple customer reports OR 2+ regions AND duration >=10-15m -> statuspage
- Else if strictly canary + isolated -> no statuspage, internal + support-only
- If we trigger load shedding -> statuspage immediately

Sean Gallagher: sounds right. Aisha can you write this into the rollout checklist doc? I’ll add to oncall runbook as “Statuspage criteria - tiered defaults rollout.”

Aisha Bello: yep. I’ll post the criteria + templates in the rollout checklist and link here. also adding a checkbox: “statuspage decision made (Y/N) + rationale” for canary steps.

Marissa Cole: last note: for any statuspage post during rollout, also post a short internal blurb in #support and #customer-success so they can handle inbound.

Connor O'Brien: + we should pre-create the incident in statuspage for fast posting? (draft saved)

Sean Gallagher: do it. but keep it unpublished until needed. ok shipping this playbook.

Aisha Bello: cool, I’ll update with final thresholds:
- FAST streaming TTFT p99 > +30% for 10m AND >5% traffic
- STANDARD e2e p95 > +20% for 30m AND >10% traffic
- ANY tier p99 > +50% for 10m in 2+ regions OR load shedding -> immediate
- 3+ support reports in 20m -> immediate review for posting

Allison Grant: works. I’ll ensure dashboards have a “traffic impacted” panel to make this fast.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the release brief too so it’s not tribal knowledge.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: hey folks — need an SLO proposal for the Cost Model API before we bless Console hot-path integration. what are we thinking for availability/latency + how we define the drift/correctness SLI? also flagging alerting risks: telemetry lag + changing schema semantics can make “drift” look like a production regression.

Ethan Park: +1, we’ve got a draft SLO doc started but it’s not reviewed. we were targeting internal tier 0.5-ish (Console depends on it but we’ll have fallback). for latency we were thinking p95 < 150ms for `estimate` and p95 < 300ms for `breakdown` (bounded responses only).

Allison Grant: before numbers: what’s the dependency graph + what’s “critical”? if Console is calling synchronously, SLO needs to reflect that OR we mandate caching/fallback and treat Cost Model API as tier-1 dependency but not page-on-everything.

Talia Benson: Console integration is behind flag + has a fallback path to the old calculator if cost-model is degraded (per Marissa/Chloe plan). but we still want strong SLOs since this will become the single source.

Rafael Mendes: got it. what I need is:
- availability target (rolling 28d)
- latency SLO (p95 or p99?) for each endpoint class
- error budget + paging policy
- definition of drift SLI + how we avoid false positives from telemetry delay

Logan Wright: on drift: we can compare predicted cost (from cost-model) vs observed spend attribution, but that observed side is NOT real-time. telemetry pipeline can be 10–45 mins behind depending on backpressure. if we page on a short window drift, we will page constantly.

Nadia Rahman: also note drift is not strictly “correctness”. there’s:
1) request-level token accounting correctness (prefill/decode split, cache hit accounting)
2) aggregation correctness (bucketing, joins, late-arriving events)
3) spend allocation policy changes (overhead allocation, reserved capacity) that can move denominators

Thomas Baird: + cardinality issues. if we crank up per-route/per-model breakdown at high qps, we’ll get timeouts that look like “latency regressions” but are actually unbounded fanout. the SLO should be tied to bounded request shapes + reject/429 the rest.

Ethan Park: re bounded shapes: API guidelines require request bounds. `breakdown` endpoint only supports `top_k` dimensions + time buckets; should hard-fail with `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT` if someone asks for unbounded group_by.

Allison Grant: ok, so for SLO we should be explicit: latency SLO applies only to requests within documented bounds. out-of-bounds requests are excluded IF they return the correct error code fast.

Rafael Mendes: that’s fine. can you propose concrete targets?

Ethan Park: proposal (v1):
- Availability: 99.9% for `estimate` + `what_if`, 99.5% for `breakdown` (more expensive dependency on telemetry store)
- Latency: p95 < 200ms for `estimate` and `what_if`, p95 < 500ms for `breakdown` (bounded), measured server-side
- Timeouts: clients use 750ms deadline for `estimate` and fallback if exceeded

Cole Summers: from billing hooks side, we can tolerate slightly higher latency but we can’t tolerate long tail timeouts during peak; timeouts cascade into retries.

Rafael Mendes: 99.9 seems OK if fallback exists, but we should align with “tiering”: if it becomes billing primitive we’ll need higher later. are we sure about 500ms p95 for breakdown? Console will call that a lot.

Thomas Baird: breakdown should be cached. if Console calls breakdown per page load with common filters, we can cache (service-side) for e.g. 30–60s keyed by (tenant, model, route, window, schema_version). otherwise you’ll get hot-spot queries.

Logan Wright: + we should discourage asking for “last 5m” breakdown if telemetry is 30m delayed. better: Console shows “data freshness” banner + uses a window that ends at now-30m.

Allison Grant: this ties into drift SLI definition. my suggestion:
- Drift SLI computed by scheduled job, not inline.
- Use a trailing window (e.g., last 24h) with a fixed lag (exclude last 2h) to allow late data.
- Alert if drift > threshold for N consecutive runs AND data freshness is healthy.

Rafael Mendes: yes. can you put that into an SLI definition? what threshold are we comfortable with?

Nadia Rahman: depends on what drift means. if we compare “predicted request-attributable cost” vs “observed GPU spend allocated”, overhead allocation will make them diverge by design unless we include the same overhead buckets.

Cole Summers: finance asked that overhead appear in outputs explicitly. so drift should probably be computed on:
- Total cost (request-attributable + allocated overhead) vs observed spend
AND separately:
- Request-attributable only vs token-attributed component of spend

Ethan Park: agreed. v1 outputs include cost buckets. drift job can compute:
A) attributable_total_usd vs observed_allocated_total_usd
B) attributable_request_usd vs observed_request_usd (where observed_request is derived from metered tokens * effective $/token, not raw cloud bill)

Logan Wright: we can do B more reliably earlier; A needs finance’s monthly allocation pipeline and will be noisier daily.

Allison Grant: for alerting, we should page only on “service health” (availability/latency/error rate). drift should be ticket + warn (Slack) unless it’s extremely high and persistent.

Rafael Mendes: +1. I don’t want oncall paged because BigQuery delayed a partition.

Thomas Baird: telemetry lag should be a separate SLI/alert: “freshness” of inputs. if freshness drops below X for Y mins, we suppress drift alerts and instead alert telemetry oncall.

Rafael Mendes: who owns telemetry freshness alert? Logan?

Logan Wright: yes, Telemetry & Analytics can own freshness dashboards/alerts, but we need to agree on the contract: what’s acceptable lag for cost-model dependencies.

Ethan Park: we can add to service overview: “drift computation uses telemetry T+2h lag; freshness SLI must be green to evaluate drift”.

Allison Grant: concrete suggestion for v1 drift SLI:
- Metric: `abs(predicted_total - observed_total) / observed_total`
- Window: 24h trailing, ending 2h before now
- Thresholds: warn > 3% for 3 consecutive runs; critical > 7% for 3 consecutive runs
- Gating: only evaluate if telemetry freshness p95 < 45m and sample size > min_requests

Rafael Mendes: this is good. add “min_requests” or “min_cost” so we don’t get noise on low-volume tenants.

Nadia Rahman: also gate on schema_version distribution. if a new schema version rolled out, we may see a step change; should annotate drift dashboards with version rollout.

Ethan Park: we can export `schema_version` as a tag in the drift job + dashboard. there’s already a plan to track version distribution.

Talia Benson: action items?

Rafael Mendes: pls:
1) Ethan/Allison: put the SLO proposal into the cost-model SLO doc (availability/latency + paging policy) by EOW.
2) Logan/Nadia: define telemetry freshness SLI + publish the contract (what’s “fresh enough” for drift) and who gets paged.
3) Ethan: add drift SLI definition + gating to the observability doc and confirm alert severity (ticket vs page).

Allison Grant: works. I’ll review the SLO doc once Ethan updates.

Logan Wright: will draft freshness SLI: something like “time since latest complete partition” + “event ingestion delay p95”. we’ll propose thresholds.

Cole Summers: pls include note that billing hooks should not block on drift correctness. billing uses primitives; drift is audit/quality.

Rafael Mendes: yep. also pls ensure runbook calls out: if drift alert fires + freshness red, treat as telemetry lag, not cost-model regression.

Ethan Park: ack. will update docs + ping for review.

Talia Benson: thanks all — I’ll track these in the program board and nudge if we slip.

source.thread:
Rafael Mendes: Starting a thread for **next game day** planning. Goal: tighter blast radius + more realistic load than the 1/15 us-east->eu-west test.

Constraints I’m proposing:
- blast radius: <= 5% of Hosted traffic, tenant allowlist only, 1 model family (chat) + 1 embeddings route max
- realistic load: use replayed prod-ish mix (prompt lengths + streaming) not just synthetic probes
- scenario: *partial* impairment (e.g., elevated 5xx + tail latency in us-east) rather than hard-kill, then confirm we don’t flap

What should we change vs last time? What should we explicitly NOT do?

Talia Benson: +1. Also: can we please pick a date/time now so I can get a calendar hold w/ exec observers? Suggest Wed 1/22 10:00–11:30 PT (low-ish traffic). If that’s too soon, Thu.

Miles Thornton: From architecture side, last game day’s main learning was we got the flip right, but the *edges* were noisy:
- latency spike in eu-west when traffic shifted faster than queue depth stabilised
- hold-down + rollback criteria were a little too aggressive when reachability jittered

For next one, I’d like to test:
1) “brownout” signal: sustained p99 + 5xx burn in primary, reachability OK
2) confirm hierarchy does **not** fail over on latency-only unless corroborated (per ADR-042)
3) verify anti-flap: we should observe stable decisioning for at least 20m after flip even if one signal goes green briefly

Allison Grant: Observability asks for the next run:
- please commit to a single source of truth for timeline markers (automation audit events preferred)
- we should add a pre-created Grafana playlist for observers (signals -> policy -> outcomes -> capacity)
- for “realistic load”, I can help build a dashboard comparing request shape pre/post shift (streaming %, ctx length buckets, completion tokens)

Also: last time we were missing an easy “in-scope traffic %” panel. I can add it but need the routing tag/label we’ll use.

Mina Haddad: Routing POV: I’m strongly in favor of **explicit route allowlist** and **policy sandbox**.
Proposal:
- allowlist: `/v1/chat/completions` on `rw-chat-3` only (whatever the current stable is)
- separate policy key: `failover_gameday_2026q1` so we don’t touch default routing policies
- `require_approval=true` stays on
- traffic shift cap: start at 1% -> 3% -> 5% (step every 5 min) only if queue depth + tail latency are within gates

If we do embeddings too, we should do it as a *second* test, not combined.

Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity: last time eu-west had headroom on paper but we saw queue depth spike when batching wasn’t as effective under shifted mix.
For next game day I want a hard gate:
- eu-west queue depth <= 1.3x baseline for 5 min before we increase from 3% -> 5%
- if queue depth > 1.7x baseline for 2 min, rollback immediately

Also: can we run a pre-test “soak” by sending the replay load to eu-west *shadow* (not serving) to validate scheduling behavior?

Peter Holtz: Gateway/circuit breaker: please don’t do a test that relies on client retries behaving nicely.
For “more realistic load”, we need to include:
- streaming disconnect/reconnect patterns (some clients still do it wrong)
- a controlled amount of 429/overload to verify `Retry-After` behavior and SDK backoff

But blast radius wise: keep this to allowlisted tenants + we should explicitly exclude long-lived streaming sessions (>60s) if possible.

Victor Alvarez: Network: I’d prefer the impairment injection not be “mess with DNS” again. That part is scary + hard to attribute.
We can simulate regional impairment via:
- traffic shaping on the us-east gateway to introduce consistent elevated error rate (5xx) for the allowlist
- or egress block from gateway -> model runtime (targeted) to create reachability failures that probes see

If we do reachability impairment, make sure perf-canary probes are in-scope of the same path (we had one mismatch last time).

Devon Price: From incident-bot/tooling:
- happy to add a “game day mode” message template (posts scope, allowlist, gates, rollback owner)
- also can auto-post the *dry-run recommendation* every 2 minutes during the window to reduce “what did it decide?” questions

But +1 to Allison: audit events should be the timeline source, not humans typing timestamps.

Tomáš Novák: Runbook/doc asks:
- we need a single page for “game day execution” that isn’t spread across Hosted failover + controller runbook + checklist
- please decide now who is the rollback authority (Rafael?) and who toggles the feature flags (routing oncall)

Also: last time the “stop conditions” were in email + slack. Let’s put them in the checklist doc at the top.

Sergio Costa: Alerting: for the next test I’d like to try a cleaner approach:
- burn-rate alerts stay enabled but are routed to `#eng-sre` only
- *separate* “game day SLO tracker” alert that fires if we exceed our customer-visible error gate (so observers see it)

We should annotate alert rules w/ the allowlist label so we don’t page on unrelated noise.

Aisha Bello: SLO reporting: can we define success criteria in terms of “availability for in-scope tenants” + “global unaffected”? I can build an SLO slice but need:
- allowlist label (tenant_tier + tenant_id list is too high cardinality)
- explicit measurement window (during test + 30m after)

If we do partial impairment, success should include: no measurable availability regression for out-of-scope traffic.

Owen Phillips: Support angle: if blast radius is truly <=5% + allowlisted, we can probably avoid proactive outbound, but we need:
- internal macro ready (“planned reliability test may cause brief errors for a small set of accounts”)
- list of allowlisted tenants shared w/ Support on-call so we can identify if an inbound is expected vs not

Also please pick a short window and stick to it. Random spillover causes confusion.

Sean Gallagher: Leadership ask: make this one boring + repeatable.
I want a run-of-show that we can run quarterly:
- exact scope definition
- exact gates + rollback triggers
- automatic report afterwards (detection time, decision time, flip time, error impact)

Also: please don’t expand scope mid-test. If we want embeddings too, schedule a separate game day.

Rafael Mendes: Great input. Draft plan (v0) based on this thread:
- Date: hold Wed 1/22 10:00–11:30 PT (Talia to schedule)
- Scope: Hosted only, chat route only, allowlisted tenants, start at 1% and step to 3% then 5% w/ capacity gates
- Impairment: partial brownout in us-east for allowlist (5xx + tail latency) via gateway shaping (Victor + Peter to propose safest mechanism)
- Success: flip decision stable (no flap), <=1% sustained errors on in-scope tenants, no measurable out-of-scope regression
- Rollback authority: Rafael. Flag changes: Mina/Ravi.

Open questions:
1) how to do “realistic load replay” safely (shadow vs live) (Allison/Vanessa?)
2) what label we use for SLO slice + dashboards without exploding cardinality (Aisha/Sergio?)

I’ll put this into the checklist doc as a new tab and share for edits.

Talia Benson: Calendar hold going out today. I’ll add: Rafael (IC), Miles (arch), Allison (obs), Victor (network), Mina (routing), Vanessa (capacity), Peter (gateway), Devon (tooling), Owen (support liaison).

Please reply in-thread if Wed doesn’t work.

Mina Haddad: Wed works. For labeling: we can add a `route_policy_tag=gameday_2026q1` at policy evaluation time (low-card) and propagate to gateway metrics. Ravi can confirm feasibility.

Ravi Menon: Yup doable. We already have `policy_id` in audit events; adding a bounded `policy_tag` metric label should be OK if we keep the vocabulary tiny (e.g., `default`, `gameday`, `incident`).

Allison Grant: For load: we can do a replay generator in staging first, then in prod use *real traffic* but only for allowlisted tenants + use the stepping Mina outlined. Shadow load in eu-west sounds good but needs isolation so we don’t burn capacity.

Vanessa Ortiz: Shadow load: we can point it at a “warmup” pool w/ hard quotas so it can’t starve real. I’ll draft a safe config.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll propose two impairment options by EOD:
A) gateway-level error injection for allowlist only
B) network ACL to specific upstream endpoints (riskier)
My bias is A.

Peter Holtz: +1 on A. And we should explicitly verify SDK behavior in advance: if we see aggressive retries, we abort at 1%.

Devon Price: I’ll add a bot template: `/gameday start` posts scope + links + gates. Also can post `/gameday end` summary stub to kick off the report.

Tomáš Novák: I’ll start the consolidated “Game day execution” doc page and link the checklist + runbook steps in one place.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. I’ll post the draft checklist tab link once it’s up. Let’s keep iterating here for another day, then we’ll move to a go/no-go thread closer to the date.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: dropping the proposed canary rollout plan + abort conditions for tiered batching defaults. want explicit “stop the train” criteria before we start moving traffic.

Rafael Mendes: dashboards draft: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching (WIP)
runbook (overload/tiered batching): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Runbook+-+Tiered+Batching+Defaults+Overload+Behavior
perf gates doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Perf+Canary+Gates+-+SLO+Tiers

Sean Gallagher: :eyes: thanks. can we make the rollout steps super concrete (percentages + dwell time + what we look at) and also define “warn” vs “abort”?

Aisha Bello: +1. also we need paging policy. not everything should page, but fast-tier p99 blowing up probably should.

Rafael Mendes: current plan (Hosted API only):
1) internal canary (employee traffic + synthetic): enable flag in 1 region, 1 model family, 1% traffic for 30m
2) external canary: 1% in 2 regions for 60m
3) ramp 5% for 2h
4) ramp 25% for 4h
5) ramp 50% for overnight (8-12h)
6) ramp 100% next business day
kill switch: `tiered_batching_defaults_enabled=false` at control plane, plus per-route override to pin legacy batching

Allison Grant: we should also include “don’t ramp during incidents / when error budget burn is already elevated” clause. last time we got bitten by unrelated deps.

Rafael Mendes: yep. adding preconditions:
- no active SEV
- 7d error budget burn < 20% for relevant SLOs (region+model)
- perf canary green for last 24h
- telemetry pipeline healthy (cardinality guardrails in place)

Caleb Johnson: telemetry pipeline note: when we add `tier=` label, panels relying on old metric names will look “empty” if folks aren’t on latest dashboards. we need to make sure the new dashboard is the default in SRE folder.

Aisha Bello: can we list exactly what dashboards must be open during each dwell?

Rafael Mendes: during dwell we watch:
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 (prefill + decode) by tier/model/region
- Error rate: 5xx, timeouts, cancellations
- Scheduler: queue depth, admission rejects, max_wait_ms hit rate
- Batch: batch_tokens distribution, effective batch size, prefill/decode balance
- Cache: prefix/KV cache hit rate (if relevant), eviction spikes
- Infra: GPU util, HBM spikes, OOMs, host throttling

Sean Gallagher: ok. now abort conditions. suggest we codify “any of these -> immediate rollback + hold” vs “any of these -> pause ramp + investigate”.

Allison Grant: also “pause ramp” should be automatic if possible (release tooling gate), but we still need human confirmation.

Paula Mendes: release automation can read a subset of metrics (latency + 5xx + queue depth) and block the next step. it can’t reason about everything (cache quirks etc). we should define the minimal gating set.

Rafael Mendes: proposal for hard abort (immediate rollback):
A) fast tier p99 > SLO target * 1.15 for 10m in any canary region for top-3 models
B) standard tier p95 > SLO target * 1.20 for 15m
C) overall 5xx > baseline + 0.3% absolute for 5m (excluding client cancels)
D) scheduler admission rejects > 2% for 10m (indicates overload/backpressure misbehaving)
E) queue depth sustained > 3x baseline for 15m AND rising
F) any GPU OOM rate > 0.1% of requests over 10m

Aisha Bello: what’s the actual SLO target numbers again? people will ask.

Sean Gallagher: those live in the tier SLO doc but for the thread we should link + summarize.

Rafael Mendes: link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Latency+SLO+Tiers+Fast+Standard+Cost-Efficient
summary (Hosted, US/EU):
- fast: p95 <= 350ms, p99 <= 700ms for short prompts; streaming: time-to-first-token p99 <= 450ms
- standard: p95 <= 900ms, p99 <= 1800ms
- cost-efficient: p95 <= 1800ms, p99 <= 3500ms (non-streaming only in v1 defaults)

Keiko Yamamoto: note: we said cost-efficient defaults won’t be applied to streaming routes at launch (still standard unless overridden). should be in abort logic too (if we see cost-efficient on streaming -> bug).

Julian Moore: +1. reason codes should tell us if a route got the wrong tier via override/policy. can we add “abort if reason_code=unexpected_tier_mapping spikes”? maybe “pause+investigate”.

Allison Grant: i’d call that “pause ramp” not “rollback” unless it’s widespread. but we should still fix quickly.

Rafael Mendes: adding soft abort (pause ramp / investigate):
- reason_code=MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK > 5% (means our defaults incompatible w/model or quant profile)
- tier label cardinality warning (metrics ingestion cost jump) > 2x baseline for 15m
- fast tier TTFT p99 regression > 10% for 15m even if overall p99 not tripped
- support tickets spike (CS channel noise) correlated w/canary region

Logan Wright: on cardinality: please don’t only look at ingestion $. look at `metrics_series_active` and `topk_labels` panel. cost lags.

Caleb Johnson: also reminder: we already have the cardinality limiter hotfix queued (the one that buckets some labels). if this canary is pre-hotfix, we’re going to get noise.

Connor O'Brien: runtime 1.22 train includes the cardinality limiter patch. release cut is tomorrow 10am PT. can we gate “external canary” on running 1.22.1+?

Rafael Mendes: yes. adding prerequisite: external canary only on runtime >= 1.22.1 (tier label limiter included).

Aisha Bello: can we specify rollback steps? “flip kill switch” is good but we need the exact order and expected time-to-recover.

Rafael Mendes: rollback runbook excerpt:
1) set `tiered_batching_defaults_enabled=false` (global)
2) verify config propagated (control plane status page; expected <2m)
3) confirm `reason_code=TIER_DEFAULT` drops to ~0 in canary regions
4) if latency doesn’t recover in 10m, also set `scheduler.max_wait_ms` back to legacy default via emergency override (runbook link)
5) post in #incidents (if customer impact) + #eng-sre summary

Sean Gallagher: can we add “abort if rollback doesn’t recover” clause? e.g. if problem persists after disabling defaults, we know it’s not the feature and we need to stop blaming it.

Rafael Mendes: yep: if after rollback p99 doesn’t return within 15m, treat as separate incident; keep defaults off until RC.

Allison Grant: question on thresholds: using “SLO target * 1.15” is fine but in some regions baseline is already close to target. can we add baseline-relative as well? like “> baseline + 15%”.

Paula Mendes: agree. absolute SLO multipliers can trip too early in noisy conditions or too late if target is loose. maybe (SLO AND baseline) combined.

Rafael Mendes: updated hard abort for fast p99:
- trigger if (p99 > target*1.15) OR (p99 > baseline*1.20) for 10m.
standard p95: (p95 > target*1.20) OR (p95 > baseline*1.25) for 15m.

Aisha Bello: paging policy:
- page on fast tier p99 abort
- ticket on standard tier p95 pause
- ticket on cost-efficient regressions unless it causes global saturation
ok?

Sean Gallagher: works. but if standard p95 spikes + 5xx also spike, that’s a page. combine signals.

Allison Grant: we should include one more guardrail: “queue depth rising + GPU util pinned + increased cancels” typically indicates overload mode not doing the right thing. might be worth a hard abort.

Julian Moore: in overload mode the scheduler will start shedding based on wait budget; if we see cancels > 1% and admission rejects rising, that’s mis-tuned.

Rafael Mendes: adding hard abort:
G) cancellations/timeouts > baseline + 0.5% absolute for 10m AND GPU util > 90% sustained (indicates overload behavior change)

Connor O'Brien: what about canary scope? which regions/models are “top-3”? we should list to avoid confusion.

Rafael Mendes: initial external canary scope:
- regions: us-east-1 + eu-west-1
- model families: Llama-3.x 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/32B (hosted top traffic)
- endpoints: chat (streaming + non-streaming), embeddings unaffected (but still monitor)

Keiko Yamamoto: can we explicitly say “don’t enable on Dedicated in this phase”? i’ve heard some folks assumed canary includes dedicated clusters.

Sean Gallagher: yes. Hosted only. Dedicated is opt-in later with a separate checklist.

Caleb Johnson: dashboard link above is WIP; the panels for queue depth by tier need the new metric name. i’ll patch today and update the link.

Allison Grant: please also add a “one-pager” in the runbook: what to look at first when fast tier p99 regresses (is it wait budget? batching too big? model constraint fallback?).

Rafael Mendes: noted. i’ll add a triage section:
- check `reason_code` split
- check `max_wait_ms_hit_rate`
- check batch_tokens distribution shift
- compare streaming vs non-streaming (TTFT vs total)
- confirm no region capacity event

Aisha Bello: last thing: comms. if we abort/rollback during business hours, do we post to status page?

Sean Gallagher: status page only if customer-visible SLO breach sustained > 15m or multiple regions. otherwise internal comms + support heads-up.

Paula Mendes: can we also wire the rollout automation to post a summary message in #eng-sre on each ramp step with links + current metrics snapshot?

Rafael Mendes: yes. will add to automation ask. for now manual template:
- “ramp to X% started”
- “dwell complete, metrics green”
- “abort triggered due to <condition>”

Logan Wright: please include the exact query links for the key graphs (latency, 5xx, queue depth). makes it faster on-call.

Rafael Mendes: will do. for now, use the dashboard + these direct panels:
- latency panel: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=12
- error panel: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=18
- queue depth: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=27

Sean Gallagher: looks good. once Caleb updates the dashboard, let’s lock this and link it from the rollout checklist doc.

Rafael Mendes: ack. i’ll incorporate edits and paste final abort/pause table in the rollout checklist by EOD.

Allison Grant: also “pause ramp” should be automatic if possible (release tooling gate), but we still need human confirmation.

Paula Mendes: release automation can read a subset of metrics (latency + 5xx + queue depth) and block the next step. it can’t reason about everything (cache quirks etc). we should define the minimal gating set.

Rafael Mendes: proposal for hard abort (immediate rollback):
A) fast tier p99 > SLO target * 1.15 for 10m in any canary region for top-3 models
B) standard tier p95 > SLO target * 1.20 for 15m
C) overall 5xx > baseline + 0.3% absolute for 5m (excluding client cancels)
D) scheduler admission rejects > 2% for 10m (indicates overload/backpressure misbehaving)
E) queue depth sustained > 3x baseline for 15m AND rising
F) any GPU OOM rate > 0.1% of requests over 10m

Aisha Bello: what’s the actual SLO target numbers again? people will ask.

Sean Gallagher: those live in the tier SLO doc but for the thread we should link + summarize.

Rafael Mendes: link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Latency+SLO+Tiers+Fast+Standard+Cost-Efficient
summary (Hosted, US/EU):
- fast: p95 <= 350ms, p99 <= 700ms for short prompts; streaming: time-to-first-token p99 <= 450ms
- standard: p95 <= 900ms, p99 <= 1800ms
- cost-efficient: p95 <= 1800ms, p99 <= 3500ms (non-streaming only in v1 defaults)

Keiko Yamamoto: note: we said cost-efficient defaults won’t be applied to streaming routes at launch (still standard unless overridden). should be in abort logic too (if we see cost-efficient on streaming -> bug).

Julian Moore: +1. reason codes should tell us if a route got the wrong tier via override/policy. can we add “abort if reason_code=unexpected_tier_mapping spikes”? maybe “pause+investigate”.

Allison Grant: i’d call that “pause ramp” not “rollback” unless it’s widespread. but we should still fix quickly.

Rafael Mendes: adding soft abort (pause ramp / investigate):
- reason_code=MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK > 5% (means our defaults incompatible w/model or quant profile)
- tier label cardinality warning (metrics ingestion cost jump) > 2x baseline for 15m
- fast tier TTFT p99 regression > 10% for 15m even if overall p99 not tripped
- support tickets spike (CS channel noise) correlated w/canary region

Logan Wright: on cardinality: please don’t only look at ingestion $. look at `metrics_series_active` and `topk_labels` panel. cost lags.

Caleb Johnson: also reminder: we already have the cardinality limiter hotfix queued (the one that buckets some labels). if this canary is pre-hotfix, we’re going to get noise.

Connor O'Brien: runtime 1.22 train includes the cardinality limiter patch. release cut is tomorrow 10am PT. can we gate “external canary” on running 1.22.1+?

Rafael Mendes: yes. adding prerequisite: external canary only on runtime >= 1.22.1 (tier label limiter included).

Aisha Bello: can we specify rollback steps? “flip kill switch” is good but we need the exact order and expected time-to-recover.

Rafael Mendes: rollback runbook excerpt:
1) set `tiered_batching_defaults_enabled=false` (global)
2) verify config propagated (control plane status page; expected <2m)
3) confirm `reason_code=TIER_DEFAULT` drops to ~0 in canary regions
4) if latency doesn’t recover in 10m, also set `scheduler.max_wait_ms` back to legacy default via emergency override (runbook link)
5) post in #incidents (if customer impact) + #eng-sre summary

Sean Gallagher: can we add “abort if rollback doesn’t recover” clause? e.g. if problem persists after disabling defaults, we know it’s not the feature and we need to stop blaming it.

Rafael Mendes: yep: if after rollback p99 doesn’t return within 15m, treat as separate incident; keep defaults off until RC.

Allison Grant: question on thresholds: using “SLO target * 1.15” is fine but in some regions baseline is already close to target. can we add baseline-relative as well? like “> baseline + 15%”.

Paula Mendes: agree. absolute SLO multipliers can trip too early in noisy conditions or too late if target is loose. maybe (SLO AND baseline) combined.

Rafael Mendes: updated hard abort for fast p99:
- trigger if (p99 > target*1.15) OR (p99 > baseline*1.20) for 10m.
standard p95: (p95 > target*1.20) OR (p95 > baseline*1.25) for 15m.

Aisha Bello: paging policy:
- page on fast tier p99 abort
- ticket on standard tier p95 pause
- ticket on cost-efficient regressions unless it causes global saturation
ok?

Sean Gallagher: works. but if standard p95 spikes + 5xx also spike, that’s a page. combine signals.

Allison Grant: we should include one more guardrail: “queue depth rising + GPU util pinned + increased cancels” typically indicates overload mode not doing the right thing. might be worth a hard abort.

Julian Moore: in overload mode the scheduler will start shedding based on wait budget; if we see cancels > 1% and admission rejects rising, that’s mis-tuned.

Rafael Mendes: adding hard abort:
G) cancellations/timeouts > baseline + 0.5% absolute for 10m AND GPU util > 90% sustained (indicates overload behavior change)

Connor O'Brien: what about canary scope? which regions/models are “top-3”? we should list to avoid confusion.

Rafael Mendes: initial external canary scope:
- regions: us-east-1 + eu-west-1
- model families: Llama-3.x 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/32B (hosted top traffic)
- endpoints: chat (streaming + non-streaming), embeddings unaffected (but still monitor)

Keiko Yamamoto: can we explicitly say “don’t enable on Dedicated in this phase”? i’ve heard some folks assumed canary includes dedicated clusters.

Sean Gallagher: yes. Hosted only. Dedicated is opt-in later with a separate checklist.

Caleb Johnson: dashboard link above is WIP; the panels for queue depth by tier need the new metric name. i’ll patch today and update the link.

Allison Grant: please also add a “one-pager” in the runbook: what to look at first when fast tier p99 regresses (is it wait budget? batching too big? model constraint fallback?).

Rafael Mendes: noted. i’ll add a triage section:
- check `reason_code` split
- check `max_wait_ms_hit_rate`
- check batch_tokens distribution shift
- compare streaming vs non-streaming (TTFT vs total)
- confirm no region capacity event

Aisha Bello: last thing: comms. if we abort/rollback during business hours, do we post to status page?

Sean Gallagher: status page only if customer-visible SLO breach sustained > 15m or multiple regions. otherwise internal comms + support heads-up.

Paula Mendes: can we also wire the rollout automation to post a summary message in #eng-sre on each ramp step with links + current metrics snapshot?

Rafael Mendes: yes. will add to automation ask. for now manual template:
- “ramp to X% started”
- “dwell complete, metrics green”
- “abort triggered due to <condition>”

Logan Wright: please include the exact query links for the key graphs (latency, 5xx, queue depth). makes it faster on-call.

Rafael Mendes: will do. for now, use the dashboard + these direct panels:
- latency panel: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=12
- error panel: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=18
- queue depth: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/batching-tiers/canary-tiered-batching?viewPanel=27

Sean Gallagher: looks good. once Caleb updates the dashboard, let’s lock this and link it from the rollout checklist doc.

Rafael Mendes: ack. i’ll incorporate edits and paste final abort/pause table in the rollout checklist by EOD.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: hey folks — need quick eyes on the customer comms draft for the planned regional failover test next week. want to lock wording + timing + escalation contacts today so Support isn’t improvising.

Rafael Mendes: draft is here (Google doc): https://drive.google.com/redwood/failover/customer-comms-draft-planned-failover

Kira Thompson: skimming now. first reaction: we say “no customer impact expected” in the first paragraph. i’d soften that.

Kira Thompson: maybe “we do not expect significant impact, but you may see brief increases in latency or transient errors during the window.”

Dev Patel: +1. Support gets burned when we use absolutes.

Owen Phillips: agree. can we also define what “brief” means? like “<2 minutes” if that’s true, or just “short-lived”.

Rafael Mendes: good call. we *hope* <60s but don’t want to promise. i can say “short-lived” and keep the quantitative bits internal.

Tomáš Novák: also the draft says “test is fully automated.” is that accurate? we still have approval hooks in prod, right?

Rafael Mendes: yep, approvals required for execution. automation does detection + recommendation; on-call acknowledges. will tweak.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should frame it as “controlled” and “with active monitoring + rollback.”

Lauren Bishop: dedicated customers will ask “are you draining my pool?” draft implies routing-only.

Lauren Bishop: can we add a Dedicated-specific line: “Dedicated capacity will remain reserved; any drain/evacuation would be performed only if required for safety and with our on-call team engaged.”

Rafael Mendes: yep. we’re not planning a drain step for Dedicated tenants in this test, but we need to preempt the question.

Nikhil Sharma: hosted API section: we mention “automatic retries will mask impact.” careful — some clients don’t retry and we shouldn’t tell them to rely on it.

Nikhil Sharma: maybe “our platform includes safeguards (routing, circuit breakers) to reduce impact” without mentioning client behavior.

Allison Grant: +1. also if we mention retries we should link to docs. otherwise it’s vague + potentially wrong.

Kira Thompson: i’ll propose edit:

Kira Thompson: 
“During this maintenance window, we will run a controlled regional failover exercise to validate our reliability systems. We do not expect significant customer impact, but you may observe short-lived increases in latency or transient HTTP 5xx/timeout errors.”

Kira Thompson: then next sentence: “If you experience sustained impact outside the window, contact Support and reference ‘Planned Failover Test’.”

Dev Patel: for escalation contacts, can we include both the support portal and the enterprise escalation alias?

Dev Patel: Support asks for:
- support@redwoodinference.com (or portal)
- for enterprise: their CSM channel + “SEV escalation” process

Owen Phillips: and internal: we should tell customers we’ll update status page if we see meaningful impact. but don’t overcommit to “every 15 minutes” unless that’s policy.

Tomáš Novák: status page cadence standard is 30m for ongoing events, right? for planned test we can do one “start” + “complete” unless issues.

Rafael Mendes: yeah. we have the template. will align with status-page copy.

Talia Benson: timing: draft says “Jan 15 10:00–10:30 PT”. is that final? i thought we were still picking between Tue/Wed based on infra signoff.

Rafael Mendes: you’re right. i’ll change to “<DATE> <TIME>” placeholders until go/no-go. but we need something to send to the enterprise list once approved.

Sean Gallagher: for enterprise proactive notify: which accounts? i’d keep to customers with multi-region routing enabled + big traffic, and any who have asked about HA.

Dev Patel: Support list suggestion: Atlas Payments (prospect but in pilot), a couple of top 10 usage hosted accounts, and any dedicated customers in us-east/eu-west.

Lauren Bishop: +1 on dedicated accounts. some will need a heads up even if no impact expected.

Marissa Cole: wording: “This test will improve regional resiliency” is good, but also say it’s part of reliability program + we run these periodically.

Marissa Cole: sets expectation that planned drills are normal.

Allison Grant: can we add a line “we will monitor success criteria and immediately rollback if thresholds exceeded” (without giving the thresholds)?

Rafael Mendes: yep.

Kira Thompson: also there’s a sentence “failover between us-east → eu-west” — might cause data residency questions.

Kira Thompson: should we add “routing respects customer data residency and regional constraints” (esp for regulated tiers).

Rafael Mendes: good catch. we can say: “Failover behavior respects configured regional boundaries; customers with regional constraints will not be routed outside allowed regions.”

Tomáš Novák: +1. and avoid naming specific regions in generic customer-facing note unless it’s targeted comms.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. for general banner/status-page, keep it generic: “one of our regions.” for targeted emails to impacted cohort, we can include details.

Owen Phillips: Support playbook ask: include “what to tell customer if they report errors during window” (ack + ask for request ids + note planned test). can we add a short FAQ?

Dev Patel: yes please. FAQ items:
- what errors might i see?
- do i need to do anything?
- how to reach support?
- will you provide report after?

Rafael Mendes: ok. Kira/Elliot can you own FAQ + copy changes?

Elliot Price: yep. i’ll update the doc and also mirror to Confluence once final so it’s easy to find.

Elliot Price: do we want to reference the public status page URL or just “our status page”? some customers paste links in tickets.

Dev Patel: include the URL. reduces back-and-forth.

Sean Gallagher: approval chain: who signs off on outbound? i assume Neha/Sean + Support lead.

Rafael Mendes: yes. we’ll do: SRE (me) + Sean + Support escalation owner (Dev) before sending. for enterprise-targeted emails, loop in CSM.

Tomáš Novák: add internal instruction: “If customer escalates as SEV1 during the window, route to #incidents + page SRE on-call; reference change window ID.”

Rafael Mendes: good. we’ll add internal-only section (not in customer email) + link it from runbook.

Allison Grant: also: mention where customers can check current region routing? Console banner mock might cover it but not sure it ships before test.

Nikhil Sharma: console banner isn’t guaranteed. i’d avoid promising console visibility. maybe “Support can confirm current mitigation status” instead.

Rafael Mendes: agreed.

Kira Thompson: i’ll make edits now. will ping here for final review.

Elliot Price: updating doc with the changes discussed + adding FAQ. ETA 30 mins.

Dev Patel: once updated, i’ll sanity-check for Support tone and add the exact escalation wording we use in tickets.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: goal: final by EOD so Talia can slot it into the go/no-go packet.

source.messages:
Miles Thornton: need to settle this: do we require manual approval hooks for prod failover controller, or go fully auto once signals trip?

Rafael Mendes: for initial rollout i strongly prefer approval in prod. we *just* got past the noisy signal issue in staging. last thing we need is a thundering herd because controller flipped twice.

Benji Okafor: +1 to approval as default, but i’d like us to be specific about what “approval” means. on-call click button? slack /ack? timeboxed hold? otherwise it becomes theater.

Mina Haddad: from routing side: full automation is the end state. manual approval doesn’t scale and we’ll miss SLOs if we wait for a human at 3am. but i’m ok with phased.

Allison Grant: FYI dashboards/alerts can support either. bigger issue is: can on-call reliably tell it’s a real region impairment vs a localized 5xx spike? if not, approval just delays and still fails.

Ravi Menon: policy engine semantics matter here. if controller is deterministic + has corroboration (reachability + burn) + hold-down timers, the risk is much lower. the most dangerous mode is partial data → it “thinks” region dead and flips.

Tomáš Novák: also paging hygiene angle: if we add an approval step, we need a clear runbook + a single pager path. otherwise we page SRE + routing + infra every time and everyone waits for everyone.

Peter Holtz: gateway behavior matters too. if we’re still retrying too aggressively, an auto flip can amplify load on the target region before autoscaling catches up. approval hook gives us a chance to pre-warm / shed.

Miles Thornton: ok, but approval adds detection→failover time. our goal was to shrink it materially. how do we reconcile?

Rafael Mendes: proposal: approval required only when blast radius > X or confidence < Y. if it’s a “hard” signal (cross-region reachability failing from multiple probes) AND burn is high, controller can auto.

Benji Okafor: that’s basically “progressive automation.” define tiers:
- Tier 0: dry-run only (emit recommendation + evidence)
- Tier 1: auto-exec for low-risk routes/tenants, approval for the rest
- Tier 2: auto-exec broadly, approval only for enterprise / residency constrained

Allison Grant: we need to define what confidence means. i can compute a “corroboration count” (e.g., 2/3 signals) and surface it. but it needs to map to actual rules.

Ravi Menon: ADR-042 already has the hierarchy: reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity. the question is: do we require *reachability* for auto exec? i vote yes.

Mina Haddad: careful: reachability probes can be flaky too (esp. if provider is doing weird DNS). but i agree it should be required for fully automatic regional flip. latency burn alone shouldn’t flip prod.

Peter Holtz: +1. latency is noisy and can be workload-dependent. 5xx burn is stronger but can still be caused by a single service. reachability from canaries + real traffic is best.

Devon Price: from incident-bot side, approval hook is extra moving parts. but we can make it sane: controller posts a summary to #incidents + #eng-sre with buttons/commands:
`/failover approve <event_id>`
`/failover deny <event_id> reason=<...>`
then it writes audit log.

Tomáš Novák: pls no “button in slack but also in console but also in cli”. choose one primary for on-call.

Benji Okafor: agree. CLI is fine for rehearsals, slack for prod ack is probably fastest.

Miles Thornton: what are the conditions to REMOVE approvals entirely? we need criteria we can tell leadership.

Rafael Mendes: measurable criteria IMO:
1) false positive rate below threshold (e.g., <1 per quarter) in prod for routes in scope
2) successful game day(s) with auto-exec enabled on limited blast radius and no manual intervention
3) rollback proven: auto rollback triggers only when impairment cleared and does not oscillate
4) capacity gates working: target region headroom >= N% and no overload cascade
5) audit + observability complete (timeline annotations, event logs)

Allison Grant: add: alerting noise tuned. if auto-exec fires but we still page 5 teams, it’s not a win.

Mina Haddad: also: deterministic policy evaluation + “missing signal behavior” fixed. we had that NPE when a signal stream dropped. approvals won’t save us if controller crashes mid-event.

Benji Okafor: per-tenant safety limits must be on before we remove approvals:
- max flips per tenant per hour
- global max flips per region per hour
- explicit denylist (residency / regulated)

Ravi Menon: and config freeze / change control. if someone edits thresholds at noon and it auto flips at 12:05, that’s on us. we should gate config changes with review + maybe a cool-down.

Peter Holtz: plus the client behavior: streaming reconnect fix is landing, but until then, auto flips can look worse than they are because clients don’t retry correctly. approval doesn’t fix but might reduce frequency.

Devon Price: re: auditability, we can log:
- trigger signals (top 3)
- decision path (which rule fired)
- action (flip/rollback/noop)
- actor (auto vs @oncall)
- blast radius estimate
and link to dashboards.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in. we’re not deciding “forever.” the reliability program expectation is phased. leadership will accept approvals initially if we can show a clear path to removing them.

Neha Kapoor: +1. also want a crisp statement: “automation should be safe-by-default.” if approvals remain indefinitely, that’s a failure. but we won’t trade safety for speed.

Miles Thornton: ok, what’s the compromise policy for Feb rollout?

Rafael Mendes: my vote:
- default approval required in prod
- auto-exec allowed only for: Hosted API, non-enterprise tiers, limited set of routes/models, AND only when reachability corroborated by >=2 probe sources + 5xx burn > fast threshold
- for Dedicated: approval always required until drain automation + capacity validation are solid

Mina Haddad: can live with that if we set a date/exit criteria. otherwise it’ll linger.

Benji Okafor: suggest we encode it as feature flags:
`failover.auto_exec.enabled=true/false`
`failover.auto_exec.allowed_tiers=starter,pro`
`failover.auto_exec.required_signals=reachability_multi,5xx_burn`
`failover.auto_exec.max_blast_radius_pct=10`

Allison Grant: if we’re doing blast radius caps, we need the metric for “traffic shifted” in near real time (per region per route). i can add a panel + alert when it exceeds cap.

Tomáš Novák: also runbook: who has authority to approve/deny? primary on-call only? do we require IC during an incident? and what about planned game days?

Devon Price: for game days, we can pre-authorize via a change ticket and allow auto-exec within a maintenance window. bot can post “pre-approved window active” message.

Peter Holtz: one more guardrail: if target region is already in overload (queue depth high / shed active), controller should refuse to flip and require manual.

Ravi Menon: yep, capacity signal should be a gate (not a trigger). “don’t fail over into fire.”

Miles Thornton: so conditions to remove approval:
- 2 successful production drills with auto-exec on limited routes
- <1 false positive over 30 days
- capacity gate + per-tenant safety limits + anti-flap timers enabled
- audit logs + dashboard annotations confirmed
- streaming reconnect fix deployed
sound right?

Rafael Mendes: add: we need an explicit rollback play: if automation flips and things worsen, on-call should be able to “pin back” and disable automation quickly.

Benji Okafor: kill switch has to be single command. like `/failover disable --scope prod`.

Devon Price: can do. bot can expose `/failover disable` and it flips the feature flag + posts confirmation.

Allison Grant: pls make sure disable action also annotates dashboards. we missed annotations last rehearsal and it was impossible to line up timelines.

Sean Gallagher: action item: Miles + Benji draft the approval policy text for ADR-043 addendum (or a short RFC) + Rafael updates runbook to reflect slack-ack flow. we’ll review in next reliability sync.

Neha Kapoor: and include the exit criteria in that doc. i want it explicit: “approvals removed when X/Y/Z.”

Miles Thornton: ack. i’ll draft today.

Tomáš Novák: once drafted, tag me. i’ll sanity-check the on-call ergonomics + paging.

Mina Haddad: same, i’ll confirm it doesn’t conflict with routing rollout flags.

Peter Holtz: and i’ll verify it aligns with gateway circuit breaker tuning. :thumbsup:

Devon Price: i’ll prototype the `/failover approve` + `/failover disable` commands in incident-bot this week and share screenshots.

Rafael Mendes: cool. also: please make “deny” require a reason string. future us will thank you.

Devon Price: done.

Miles Thornton: closing loop: we’re not choosing manual forever; we’re choosing a ramp with explicit criteria. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: +1 to approval as default, but i’d like us to be specific about what “approval” means. on-call click button? slack /ack? timeboxed hold? otherwise it becomes theater.

Mina Haddad: from routing side: full automation is the end state. manual approval doesn’t scale and we’ll miss SLOs if we wait for a human at 3am. but i’m ok with phased.

Allison Grant: FYI dashboards/alerts can support either. bigger issue is: can on-call reliably tell it’s a real region impairment vs a localized 5xx spike? if not, approval just delays and still fails.

Ravi Menon: policy engine semantics matter here. if controller is deterministic + has corroboration (reachability + burn) + hold-down timers, the risk is much lower. the most dangerous mode is partial data → it “thinks” region dead and flips.

Tomáš Novák: also paging hygiene angle: if we add an approval step, we need a clear runbook + a single pager path. otherwise we page SRE + routing + infra every time and everyone waits for everyone.
