source.thread:
Sean Gallagher: quick check before we send the exec + wider eng update — did we meet the game day success criteria? (failover time, EB burn, customer impact). drop metrics + any caveats here pls

Rafael Mendes: yep, pulling the final numbers now. high level: met time-to-failover + rollback readiness, small miss on eu-west p99 for ~6-7 mins while headroom settled. no sustained customer impact, but we did see a handful of real 5xxs (not just synthetic).

Allison Grant: dashboards links for receipts:
- Multi-region availability SLO view (Hosted): https://console.redwood/obs/d/slo-mr-availability?from=...&to=...
- Failover signals (reachability/error burn/latency burn/capacity): https://console.redwood/obs/d/failover-signals-us-east
- Policy flips + audit events: https://console.redwood/obs/d/failover-audit

Allison Grant: criteria vs actual (Hosted API, us-east -> eu-west):
1) Detection (authoritative trigger) <= 90s: 52s (reachability probe + 5xx burn corroboration)
2) Policy flip executed <= 2 min after detection: 1m 11s (approval hook ack included)
3) End-to-end “customer-visible failover” <= 5 min: ~2m 45s (measured at gateway region header shift + traffic mix)
4) Error budget burn: within 1% daily EB: ~0.34% of daily EB for Hosted MR-Avail (most burn in first 3 mins)
5) Customer impact: no enterprise escalations; Support saw 2 inbound pings + 1 ticket correlated to test window

Sergio Costa: adding more detail on errors:
- peak 5xx rate on Hosted gateway: 0.62% over 1-min window; 15-min avg stayed <0.1%
- timeouts peaked same time as policy flip (client retries + old region drain)
- after retry-after changes, we saw clients back off better than last rehearsal (still not perfect)

Miles Thornton: from the controller perspective: success criteria mostly green. dry-run recommendation matched executed action (same target region + same reason codes). audit event looked good:
`failover_reason=reachability_loss` + `corroboration=5xx_burn` + `hold_down=900s`.

Miles Thornton: caveat: we still had one “soft oscillation” attempt (controller proposed a revert at T+12m) but anti-flap blocked it due to hold-down timer. that’s *correct behavior*, but it confused observers since the UI only showed the blocked action in logs, not on the timeline.

Aisha Bello: SLO math check: using the MR availability definition (any region-level event affecting >X% of traffic counts), this did count as a regional event for Hosted during the controlled window, but EB burn stayed within the allowed game day budget. we should annotate the SLO report so it’s clear this was planned.

Mina Haddad: routing health: traffic shifted cleanly. biggest spike was eu-west tail latency because we were right on the edge of capacity headroom for the larger context-length bucket. autoscaling caught up in ~6 mins. we should update “safe to failover” gating to include seq-len weighted headroom, not just aggregate tokens/sec.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. capacity signal was based on overall GPU queue depth + admitted tokens/sec. it didn’t reflect the long-context mix change. we can add a guardrail: require headroom >= N% for p95 context bucket before allowing full flip for Hosted tier-1.

Ravi Menon: policy engine behaved deterministically as expected. no weirdness in signal priority eval. one note: reachability probe lagged ~15s because of TTL cache jitter; still ok but worth noting for “best possible” detection time.

Peter Holtz: gateway / overload behavior: retry-after headers were issued for ~3 mins post-flip; clients that honored it looked great. clients that didn’t still hammered for a short period. we should add docs + SDK hinting (and consider default client behavior) but that’s post-project.

Dev Patel: Support summary: SUP-25310 got 3 customer messages (same account) about transient 5xx. we responded with planned test explanation + recommended retry/backoff. no further follow-ups. no Dedicated escalations.

incident-bot: Event summary: Controlled Failover Test (us-east→eu-west)
- Start: 1768610200
- Detection: 1768610252
- Policy flip: 1768610323
- Traffic majority shift: 1768610366
- Stabilized: 1768610705
Links: failover audit, signals dashboard, SLO view

Sean Gallagher: this is good. can we say “success criteria met” with explicit exception on eu-west p99? (so we don’t oversell)

Rafael Mendes: yes — recommend wording: “met primary success criteria; observed transient eu-west tail latency due to headroom modeling; no sustained customer impact; EB burn within planned budget.”

Allison Grant: + we should capture the exact p99 deviation: eu-west p99 peaked at 4.8s (target 3.0s) for 7 mins on chat/completions, mostly long-context. p95 stayed under target.

Miles Thornton: action items to attach to the update:
- add timeline annotations for *blocked* rollback proposals (so observers aren’t spelunking logs)
- improve capacity gating for long-context mix
- ensure status page / internal comms includes “planned test” tag so on-call doesn’t treat it as unplanned burn

Sean Gallagher: cool. i’ll send exec note with those bullets. please make sure we update the game day results doc + tag the SLO report as planned.

Sergio Costa: i’ll file a quick ticket for annotation panel + add a dashboard note template for planned tests.

Aisha Bello: i’ll handle SLO report annotation + confirm EB calculation for the postmortem table.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop here: ✅ “success criteria met” (with p99 caveat). next step is expanding blast radius only after capacity gating fix + annotations land. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: heads up — we got paged 6 times overnight on the new multi-region burn-rate rules. mostly recovered by the time someone opened the dash. feels noisy.

PagerDuty: Triggered: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west) — https://pagerduty.redwood.ai/incidents/PQ7K2

Tomáš Novák: yeah, that was me at 02:14. opened the failover dash and it looked green-ish except a small blip on `/v1/chat.completions`. still paged.

Allison Grant: can someone paste the alert annotation from the firing? want to see which clause tripped (fast vs slow window).

Sergio Costa: from Alertmanager:
```
alertname=multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast
scope=hosted
route_group=default
region=eu-west
window=5m
burnrate=17.8
threshold=14
```
Also slow window was *not* firing.

Miles Thornton: 17.8x over 5m with slow not corroborating sounds like either a brief 5xx spike or a partial gateway flap. We said in ADR-042 we shouldn’t page on single-window spikes w/o corroboration.

Aisha Bello: +1. for multiwindow we should require 5m+30m (or 5m+1h) both burning before paging. right now the fast one pages on its own.

Rafael Mendes: yep. current rule is basically:
- fast: 14x over 5m => page
- slow: 6x over 30m => page
(no AND)

Allison Grant: that’s the old template behavior. I think when we rushed the rule bundle we lost the multiwindow gating. We also added region label + route_group label so it’s a lot of potential alert instances.

Sergio Costa: fwiw last night was also a deploy to `api-gateway` in eu-west + perf-canary was flapping. possible causal.

Observability Bot: Grafana snapshot (auto): Failover Signals / eu-west / Hosted
- 5xx rate peaked at 1.9% for ~3 min
- p99 latency spiked to 4.2s for ~4 min
- reachability probes: OK
https://grafana.redwood.ai/s/failover-eu-west-1768634

Tomáš Novák: yep that matches what I saw. by the time I joined, error rate was back <0.2%. paging on a 3 min blip is rough.

Miles Thornton: agreed. we want fast detection for real regional impairment, but a 3-min spike shouldn’t wake someone unless it’s sustained / corroborated.

Allison Grant: proposal:
1) switch paging to multiwindow AND for “fast”: (14x over 5m) AND (6x over 30m)
2) keep (6x over 30m) as ticket-only if it fires alone
3) add 10m hold-down before auto-resolve so we don’t get fire/resolve spam

Sergio Costa: +1, and also consider grouping — page at region level, not per `route_group`. route_group should be in annotations/dashboard link only.

Rafael Mendes: question: do we still need per-model/route granularity anywhere? feels like cardinality trap.

Aisha Bello: we should keep per-route in *metrics* (for debugging) but alerts should aggregate to “region impairment candidate”. In PromQL that means something like `sum without(route_group, model)`.

Allison Grant: yep. current expression is `sum by (region, route_group) (...)`.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add a “don’t page if under N rps” guard? some of the staging-ish tenants cause high burn on tiny denominators.

Sergio Costa: careful — for SLO burn, denominator is requests, so low volume can be weird. We can clamp by requiring `requests_total > X` in the window. Alternatively, page only on `scope=prod` + `tier in (hosted, dedicated)` and exclude `sandbox`.

Rafael Mendes: prod only is fine. This was prod though. But I like the idea of a min traffic gate so a single tenant doesn’t dominate.

Miles Thornton: for regional impairment detection, a min-traffic gate makes sense, but we also need to catch “everything dead” scenarios where traffic drops to ~0 because clients can’t connect.

Allison Grant: right — if traffic goes to zero due to reachability, the burn metric won’t even move. That’s why reachability is higher priority anyway. So min-traffic gate on burn alerts is ok as long as reachability is paging separately.

Sergio Costa: reachability alerts are separate (perf-canary + external probe). Those did not fire.

Devon Price: fyi incident-bot summary posts when failover controller flips routes. last night there were no flips. so the page was purely signal noise.

Rafael Mendes: ok so concrete changes:
- multiwindow AND for paging
- aggregate out route_group
- add hold-down + maybe min traffic
Can someone own a PR to the alert bundle? (observability-pack)

Sergio Costa: I can take it. will update rules in `observability-pack/pr-907` follow-up. target today.

Allison Grant: please include before/after replay using yesterday’s data if you can (use the simulate endpoint recordings?). we should verify we still would have paged on the jan 15 real impairment.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll run it against the recorded timeseries from 2026-01-15 + last night’s blip.

Tomáš Novák: also paging routing: can we downgrade slow window to non-page (Slack + ticket) by default? on-call can escalate if it’s trending.

Miles Thornton: +1. for failover automation, we care about fast confirmable signals. slow burn is for “things are bad, look soon” not wakeups.

Aisha Bello: suggested mapping:
- fast AND (multiwindow) => PagerDuty P1
- slow only => Slack #oncall-sre + Jira ticket
- annotations include direct link to “region health evaluation logs” (PR-19835?)

Allison Grant: love that. also add runbook link in annotations: `hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook`.

Sergio Costa: ack. Draft rule shape:
```
# page only if fast + slow
(BR_5m > 14) and (BR_30m > 6)
# aggregate by region, scope
sum without(route_group, model, tenant) (...)
# min traffic
and (requests_30m > 5000)
```
Plus keep reachability separate.

Rafael Mendes: `requests_30m > 5000` seems high for some smaller regions at night. can we do per-region baseline? or just lower?

Aisha Bello: maybe `> 1000` and we can tune. also we can make it “or dedicated_pool_traffic > 200” since dedicated has lower rps but high impact.

Miles Thornton: keep it simple: start with 1000 and watch.

Allison Grant: +1. and add a note that if traffic is low, we rely more on reachability + latency + control plane health.

PagerDuty: Resolved: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west)

Tomáš Novák: lol resolved while we’re here.

Sergio Costa: ok I’ll ship changes w/ feature flag in alert bundle so we can roll out without surprises.

Rafael Mendes: cool. pls post diff + new thresholds here before merge.

---

Sergio Costa: update: pushed branch `failover-burn-multiwindow-gated`.
Changes:
- paging condition now requires BOTH 5m and 30m burn
- removed `route_group` from grouping
- slow-only now routes to Slack + ticket
- annotations include runbook + dashboard link
PR incoming in ~30.

Allison Grant: can you paste the new alert annotation text? want to ensure it explains the evidence.

Sergio Costa: annotation draft:
"Multi-region availability burn indicates possible regional impairment. Evidence: 5m burn={{$value_fast}}, 30m burn={{$value_slow}}, req_30m={{$req}}. Check Failover Signals dashboard + Region Health logs. Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/.../hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook"

Devon Price: can we also add link to incident-bot thread search? like `slack://channel?team=...&id=incidents` isn’t great. maybe just link to incident timeline tool.

Sergio Costa: I’ll link to incident timeline in console: https://ops.redwood.ai/timeline?region={{$labels.region}}

Aisha Bello: did the replay test show it would have paged on jan 15?

Sergio Costa: yes. jan 15 had sustained 5xx for ~18m, both windows burned, would page. last night would NOT page (only 5m spiked).

Miles Thornton: perfect.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: merge once approved. and let’s add a follow-up task to revisit thresholds after next gameday.

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please add to paging hygiene notes: “single-window spikes should not page” so we don’t regress later.

Allison Grant: I’ll add a short section to the dashboard spec + runbook.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. closing loop: goal is fewer wakeups but still detect real regional events fast. this seems aligned.

source.content:
Miles Thornton: Proposal coming out of gameday retro: we should make controlled regional failover tests a *quarterly* cadence (not “when we remember”). Goal is to keep automation + runbooks + signals honest.

Miles Thornton: Rough outline:
1) Quarterly controlled failover per env:
- Staging: monthly mini-drill (dry-run + simulate)
- Prod: quarterly controlled failover w/ limited blast radius
2) Ownership model:
- SRE owns schedule + run-of-show + go/no-go
- Routing/Control Plane owns configs + controller changes
- Observability owns reporting pack + dashboards
- Infra owns capacity/drain readiness
3) Automated reporting expectations:
- Auto-generated timeline + key metrics + links, posted to thread + Confluence
- Audit log export for “who/what/why”

Miles Thornton: Would like to align on: (a) cadence, (b) who is DRI, (c) what “reporting” must include so we don’t hand-write it at 2am.

Rafael Mendes: +1 to quarterly. The pain last week was less the flip and more the “did we actually meet success criteria?” scramble.

Rafael Mendes: For DRI I’d vote SRE (me/Miles) for end-to-end, but each team has explicit “readiness signoff” checklist item.

Sean Gallagher: Agree on cadence. Also want this to be part of Reliability Program KPIs (not optional). If we can’t safely fail over quarterly, we don’t actually have automated failover.

Talia Benson: I can own the calendar + project management piece (quarterly scheduling, invite list, prereq tracking). Need clarity on what quarters mean (fixed windows?)

Miles Thornton: Suggest fixed windows (e.g., 2nd week of Mar/Jun/Sep/Dec) + one alternate date. Similar to patch Tuesday mentality.

Allison Grant: On reporting: we should define a “game day report” template that is auto-filled from metrics + audit events. Minimum sections:
- start/end time, intended scenario, actual scenario
- detection time, decision time, execution time
- impact (5xx, p95/p99), SLO burn estimate
- policy flips + rollback events
- capacity headroom before/after
- follow-ups + owners

Sergio Costa: +1. If we bake this into the observability pack, we can also auto-annotate Grafana with `failover_event_id` so charts are aligned.

Aisha Bello: For SLO burn: we can run the same multi-region availability SLO calculator we’re using for incident reviews. But need consistent labels. We *can’t* explode cardinality again :grimacing:

Mina Haddad: Re: staging monthly mini-drill — make it “dry-run only” by default, plus `simulate` endpoint replay from recorded signals. That will catch regressions in priority logic without touching traffic.

Ethan Park: Control plane side: I’m fine committing to quarterly prod, but we need a hard “don’t run if control plane is degraded” gate. Also should avoid overlapping with major releases.

Tomáš Novák: From oncall perspective: +1 but we need a strict “pager impact” policy. If this triggers paging for unrelated teams every quarter, we’ll get pushback.

Rafael Mendes: We can treat it like a planned change w/ scoped paging routing. Still want pages for the teams actually running it (SRE + routing + infra), but not the entire company.

Devon Price: For automated reporting, incident-bot can post a summary when the controller enters “failover test mode” + when it completes. That message can include links to dashboards + the generated report doc.

Vanessa Ortiz: Dedicated capacity: quarterly in prod is OK if we have a dedicated “safe tenant set” and clear drain rates. I want a pre-flight check: `eu-west headroom >= X%` and `drain ETA <= Y`.

Keiko Yamamoto: + pre-flight should include autoscaler guardrails. During last drill, scale-up lag + retry storm made the tail look worse than it needed to.

Victor Alvarez: Networking angle: I’d like at least one quarterly scenario to include reachability degradation (not full blackhole). Otherwise we’re only testing the clean “region dead” case.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated customers will ask about this. Need a consistent line: “controlled tests are limited to a small subset / no customer impact expected, and Dedicated is protected via drain workflow + isolation.” Also we should ensure comms playbook has an option to proactively notify specific accounts.

Nikhil Sharma: For Hosted API, I’d prefer we do *partial* traffic shift vs full, at least initially. E.g., 5% of a small model route. That still validates end-to-end without scaring support.

Miles Thornton: +1. My mental model:
- Q1/Q2: partial shift (5–10%) on “gameday route” + limited tenants
- Q3+: expand route coverage + allow full shift in controlled window if success criteria met

Sergio Costa: Reporting pack idea: one “gameday report” job that pulls:
- controller audit events (recommended/executed/rolled back)
- region health snapshots (inputs that triggered)
- SLO burn summary
- dedicated drain progress metrics (if applicable)
And outputs a single permalink.

Aisha Bello: We should explicitly call out success criteria thresholds in the report (not implicit). Example:
- detection <= 60s
- failover execution <= 120s
- added 5xx <= 0.5% for scoped traffic
- p99 increase <= 2x for <= 10 min
If we change these, it should be an ADR/update, not ad-hoc.

Rafael Mendes: Can we make “go/no-go” a checklist with hard blockers? e.g.,
- dry-run output matches expected action in last 7 days
- dashboards + annotations verified in staging
- rollback path tested in staging
- capacity headroom green
- oncall staffing confirmed

Talia Benson: I’ll turn that into a one-pager + checklist. Also: do we want an exec sponsor to sign off each quarter or only for the first 1–2?

Sean Gallagher: First 2 in prod should require explicit approval (Sean+Neha or delegate). After we have 2 clean quarters, can move to “notify-only unless risk flags are red”.

Allison Grant: FYI on the “notify-only” model: we can have the report auto-post in #eng-sre + a weekly rollup in reliability program channel. That keeps visibility without the manual overhead.

Devon Price: If we create a `gameday_id`, incident-bot can also auto-open a lightweight incident timeline object (internal only) so the postmortem template is half-filled.

Tomáš Novák: Please also include “what pages fired during the test” in the report. If we triggered random burn alerts, we need to tune.

Mina Haddad: Routing config side: we’ll need a formal “gameday config pack” that is versioned. No one should be editing prod policies by hand in the moment.

Ethan Park: +1. We can gate it behind a feature flag + require the controller to be in “test mode” for those packs.

Vanessa Ortiz: Dedicated: we need to document “do not run gameday if eu-west GPU spot interruption is elevated” type conditions. We’ve had weird weeks.

Victor Alvarez: For network scenario, I can provide a controlled impairment method in staging. For prod we should be careful — maybe rely on synthetic probe failure injection rather than real network changes.

Miles Thornton: OK, action items:
- Talia: draft quarterly cadence doc + checklist + proposed calendar windows
- Allison/Sergio/Aisha: define reporting MVP fields + where it lives (dashboard + doc)
- Devon: incident-bot integration sketch (start/end summary + links)
- Mina/Ethan: propose “gameday config pack” versioning + feature flag story
- Vanessa/Keiko: pre-flight capacity/autoscaling gates
- Victor: propose “reachability degradation” scenario that doesn’t require risky prod network tweaks

Miles Thornton: I’ll open a Linear issue to track this as follow-up to gameday (thinking chaos test scheduler / quarterly cadence).

Talia Benson: Please tag me on the issue. Also need a single DRI for “quarterly program” overall — I’ll put Miles as technical DRI, Rafael as operations DRI?

Rafael Mendes: Works for me.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Add me as stakeholder. Let’s make sure this doesn’t die after Q1.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: will start a draft report schema in the observability pack. Also will make sure we have annotation write perms locked down.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync w/ Aisha on SLO burn calculation + label strategy so we don’t add cardinality. We can keep `failover_reason` as a bounded enum.

Devon Price: I’ll prototype bot message format:
- “Failover test started (gameday_id=…)”
- “Controller executed policy flip …”
- “Failover test completed” + report link

Tomáš Novák: Can we also include “how to opt-out” for a team? Like if a route is in a sensitive state. Might be as simple as “route allowlist” for gameday.

Mina Haddad: Yup, allowlist is the right model. No one-off exceptions.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll post the Linear link once it’s created.

Slackbot: Reminder set by Miles Thornton: “Post Linear tracking link for quarterly failover cadence” in 30 minutes.

Miles Thornton: Linear is up (ENG-2424). Dropping it here so folks can add their sub-tasks. Thanks all.

source.thread:
Talia Benson: Go/no-go for today’s controlled failover (us-east -> eu-west). Please reply with status + blockers by 10:15 PT. Goal is a crisp decision + explicit approvals. Checklist doc: https://drive.google.com/.../failover-test-checklist-sheet

Rafael Mendes: SRE side: ✅ on-call staffing, bridge ready, runbook reviewed. Remaining: confirm dashboards have annotations + final alert quieting (paging routes). No hard blockers.

Miles Thornton: Architecture: signal hierarchy + hold-down timers are in place (ADR-042/043). Dry-run has been running since yesterday w/ expected recs. Biggest risk is still signal noise (reachability spikes) causing early flip; mitigation is require corroboration (reachability + 5xx burn) + 2m dampening.

Allison Grant: Observability: dashboards deployed + saved views.
- Failover Signals: https://grafana/.../failover-signals
- Policy flips + audit events: https://grafana/.../failover-audit
Alerts: burn-rate rules are enabled but paging only to #eng-sre for test window (no PagerDuty unless manual escalate). ✅

Victor Alvarez: Network: reachability probes from perf-canary look clean in both dirs.
`us-east -> eu-west` p50 38ms / p95 62ms over last 30m.
`eu-west -> us-east` p50 41ms / p95 70ms.
DNS TTL changes are already rolled out (5m) w/ no anomalies. :thumbsup:

Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity check: eu-west headroom is OK for the planned blast radius.
- Hosted: +28% spare tokens/s vs baseline (w/ conservative batching)
- Dedicated: only 2 test tenants in scope; drain automation disabled by default
We should keep the traffic shift target <= 15% and watch queue depth. If GPU queue depth > 2x baseline for 3m, rollback.

Peter Holtz: Gateway/circuit breaker config is staged and ready.
- retries capped (1) on 5xx during the window
- `Retry-After` enabled on overload responses
We’re good as long as clients back off; known risk is some SDKs retry aggressively on streaming disconnect. (ENG-2422 fix is already in prod, but older SDKs exist.)

Tomáš Novák: Runbook gaps from rehearsal are patched. I added explicit ownership: “routing flip decision = SRE lead (Rafael), policy config = routing oncall (Mina/Ravi), network triage = Victor.”
Hosted API runbook link: https://confluence/.../hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook

Devon Price: Incident-bot updates are live. When failover executes or rolls back, bot will post:
- reason + evidence signals
- affected routes/models
- links to Grafana + audit log
Also status-page draft is queued but will NOT auto-post (manual approval only). ✅

Mina Haddad: Routing: feature flags currently:
- `failover_controller_enabled=false` (prod)
- `failover_controller_dry_run=true`
- `failover_controller_require_approval=true`
I can flip enablement for *test tenants only* once we get go. Policy evaluation deterministic changes are in (PR-19812). No schema drift.

Ravi Menon: +1. Also: per-tenant safety limits set to max 2 flips/hr, and global rate limit 1 action/min. Anti-flap hold-down 10m. Rollback requires 3 consecutive “healthy” evaluations over 5m OR manual.

Ethan Park: Control plane: no deploys in the last 12h. Change freeze in effect. Region health service is stable; cache TTL jitter landed (PR-19825). One note: if a signal stream is missing, controller won’t crash anymore (PR-19840). ✅

Irene Choi: Infra signoff: DNS + anycast steering changes are within the risk envelope we discussed. Monitoring plan looks good. No objections from infra.

Rafael Mendes: Quick risk review (SRE):
1) False positive -> unwanted flip: mitigated by corroboration + approval hook.
2) Failover causes overload in eu-west: mitigated by cap at 15% shift + shedding + clear rollback triggers.
3) Confusing comms: comms owner = Devon + Owen (Support on standby). Status page manual only.
4) Dedicated customers: only test tenants; no broad drain.
Any other risks to call out?

Victor Alvarez: Only thing: cloud provider status page has a minor “network maintenance” note in us-east but it’s marked resolved. I don’t see symptoms. If reachability goes weird, we should trust probes > provider page.

Allison Grant: Dashboard annotations: automation-driven markers are now enabled. If we do manual flips, please use `/annotate failover <reason>` in #eng-sre so timelines aren’t a mess later.

Talia Benson: Confirming success criteria from leadership email:
- detection + approval + flip in < 7 min (for the synthetic impairment)
- no sustained customer-visible error rate > 1% on in-scope tenants
- rollback ready within 2 min if thresholds hit
If we can’t meet safety gates, we stop.

Sean Gallagher: From reliability leadership: I’m comfortable proceeding given approval hooks + constrained scope. Conditions:
- keep blast radius to pre-approved tenant list
- Rafael has authority to abort at any time
- post-event writeup within 24h
:thumbsup:

Neha Kapoor: Go from me with the same conditions. Keep it boring. If anything looks off (latency tail, queue depth, unexpected 5xx), choose safety and rollback. Please ensure Support has the “planned test” language handy in case we get inbound.

Rafael Mendes: Acknowledged. Abort authority noted. We will start with dry-run snapshot posted in thread + final checklist line-by-line on the bridge.

Mina Haddad: Standing by to enable controller for test tenants once Rafael gives the word. Will post config diff before/after.

IncidentBot: (auto) Change window reminder: Controlled failover test window starts in 20 minutes. Owner: @Rafael Mendes. Observers: @Miles Thornton @Allison Grant @Victor Alvarez @Vanessa Ortiz. Runbook: https://confluence/.../routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook

Talia Benson: Decision recorded: ✅ GO. Approvers: Sean + Neha. Start time unchanged. Please move live coordination to the incident thread once it starts.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks all. Next update will be in #incidents thread when we begin execution.

source.content:
Rafael Mendes: alright, dumping post-rehearsal action items here while it’s fresh. this is from today’s staging failover rehearsal (routing controller in dry-run + synthetic impair). pls reply w/ owner + ETA.

Rafael Mendes: Top findings:
1) Signal delays / staleness
- perf-canary reachability results were ~2–4m behind real behavior (ingestion lag + scrape interval)
- region-health-service cache TTL is too sticky during partial impair → controller keeps “healthy” longer than it should
- capacity headroom signal updates are bursty; we saw a ~90s gap where routing kept thinking headroom was fine

2) Metric gaps / hard to answer “is this real?”
- dashboards missing a single panel that correlates: reachability + 5xx burn + tail latency burn + policy flips
- no automation-driven annotations, so replaying the timeline was painful
- failover controller logs don’t include the decisive evidence set (which signals actually tripped)

3) Controller behavior / guardrails
- dry-run output is close but not usable for on-call yet (needs confidence + evidence + ‘would-flip’ summary)
- anti-flap hold-down looked too aggressive for a short blip (we got stuck in “degraded” for ~10m)

4) Client impact / gateway
- we still saw retry amplification in one path (429/503 not consistently including `Retry-After`)
- streaming reconnect behavior improved vs last week but we still saw 1-2 disconnects when redirect happened mid-stream

Rafael Mendes: Proposed action items (initial):
A) Reduce signal staleness
- [ ] Tune region health cache TTL + add jitter to avoid stampede (Miles/Jordan?)
- [ ] perf-canary: publish faster + separate “probe_down” vs “region_down” semantics (Victor?)
- [ ] capacity: confirm update cadence + add “last_updated_age” metric for headroom stream (Vanessa?)

B) Make dashboards usable for game day
- [ ] add annotations on policy flips + dry-run recommendations (Allison/Devon)
- [ ] add a single “regional impairment” dashboard view w/ required panels (Sergio/Aisha)
- [ ] burn alerts: reduce noise in staging; keep fast-detect in prod (Allison)

C) Dry-run UX
- [ ] dry-run output must include: recommended action, confidence, evidence signals + timestamps, blast radius summary (Benji/Jordan)

D) Gateway / clients
- [ ] ensure `Retry-After` set consistently on overload responses (Peter)
- [ ] collect more streaming redirect logs + verify fix coverage (Mina?)

Rafael Mendes: Goal is to have these closed or mitigated before prod game day go/no-go. if you think anything is miscategorized, say so.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: on TTL/jitter. I can take A1.
We saw region-health-service caching `healthy` for 60s and then controller’s dampening adds another 120s. net effect too slow.
Action: drop TTL to 10s in “suspect” mode + add jitter. I’ll open a patch + roll into staging preset.
ETA: tomorrow EOD for PR.

Jordan Lee: +1. the deterministic evaluation did what it was told, but the inputs are stale.
For dry-run evidence set, I can add an “evaluation trace” struct we emit in logs + audit event (even in dry-run).
Needs a schema decision: keep it bounded (top N signals) to avoid log spam.

Benji Okafor: I’ll own the dry-run “on-call usable” format.
Proposal:
- `action`: failover | hold | rollback
- `confidence`: low/med/high (derived from corroboration count + signal strength)
- `evidence`: reachability, 5xx burn, latency burn, capacity, control-plane
- `constraints`: tenant flip limit, hold-down active, approval required
We can wire into CLI later but at least make the JSON log consistent.
ETA: Fri.

Allison Grant: for dashboards/annotations:
- we need controller to emit an annotation event we can ingest (same as deploy markers)
- right now, policy flips show up only as config diffs; no explicit “why”
I can coordinate w/ Devon on incident-bot + observability pack updates.
Also: burn alerts were paging in staging b/c the fast-burn rule is using low traffic denominators. We should gate paging by env + min request volume.

Devon Price: yep, I can add a small hook from controller audit events → incident-bot summary post in #incidents + annotation marker.
Need a stable link to the dashboard + runbook.
ETA: early next week unless the audit schema changes again.

Sergio Costa: I’ll build the single dashboard view.
Ask: what are the “must have” panels for go/no-go?
I’m thinking:
- reachability probes (per region pair)
- 5xx burn (1m/5m) + annotation lines
- p95/p99 latency burn
- capacity headroom + queue depth
- policy evaluation outcome (dry-run + exec) + flip rate

Aisha Bello: Must-have for SLO check:
- multi-region availability SLI (global + per route)
- error budget burn over rehearsal window
- separate Hosted API vs Dedicated pools (otherwise we’ll argue w/ ourselves for an hour)
I can add the SLI widgets + link to the SLO template.

Victor Alvarez: perf-canary lag: we’re scraping every 60s + pushing to the aggregator w/ another batch interval.
I can cut scrape to 15s in staging and publish a “freshness” gauge so region health can weigh stale results.
Also, semantics: probe failure should not immediately mean region down unless corroborated.
ETA: I can get a PR up by Thurs.

Vanessa Ortiz: capacity stream gap is on us. we’re emitting on autoscaler tick + only when it changes.
I’ll add:
- a periodic heartbeat metric (even if unchanged)
- `capacity_headroom_last_updated_seconds`
Also we should set a hard rule: if capacity signal stale > X, controller must treat as “unknown”, not “healthy”.
ETA: Fri.

Peter Holtz: gateway side: `Retry-After` inconsistency is because one code path returns 503 from upstream and bypasses overload middleware.
I’ll patch to normalize overload responses + add a test.
ETA: Thurs.

Mina Haddad: streaming disconnects: can you share client logs? in rehearsal I saw one case where we redirected and the client retried non-idempotently.
I’m aligned that we need docs guidance, but we should also make redirect response include an idempotency hint.
I can pair w/ Peter on gateway response headers.
ETA: investigate tomorrow; patch by Mon.

Tomáš Novák: runbook gap from rehearsal: on-call didn’t know whether to trust dry-run output or wait for dashboard burn.
We need a clear decision tree:
1) confirm reachability failures + corroborating 5xx burn
2) check capacity in target region
3) only then approve automation / flip
I’ll draft a runbook addendum + review w/ Rafael.
ETA: Fri draft.

incident-bot: (FYI) Rehearsal note: no incident was declared; timeline markers were not captured. Consider using a lightweight “event” object for game day to auto-collect milestones.

deploy-bot: staging: `failover-controller` deployed (rev 6b1c2f9) to `staging-us` (canary=10%).

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. Sergio/Aisha: must-have panels list looks right.
Also adding one more action item:
- [ ] controller should emit a “decision summary” metric/event even in dry-run: `would_flip_from`, `would_flip_to`, `reason_bucket` (bounded), `confidence`
This is what we’ll use for annotations + timeline.

Jordan Lee: I can implement that in the policy engine evaluation output (reason_bucket must be low-cardinality). We should keep buckets like: reachability, error_burn, latency_burn, capacity, control_plane.
No per-tenant IDs in labels.

Allison Grant: +1000 please keep reason_bucket bounded. our last attempt blew up cardinality w/ free-form reasons.

Benji Okafor: for “confidence”: we should make it deterministic, not vibes. e.g.
- high = reachability_down + (5xx burn OR latency burn)
- med = 5xx burn + latency burn (no reachability)
- low = single signal only
We can tune later.

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. Talia can you track these as a checklist + ping if anything slips past go/no-go?

Talia Benson: yep. I’ll mirror into the ENG-2421 game day issue + tag owners/ETAs. pls keep updates in-thread here too so we don’t lose context.

Rafael Mendes: final note: if anything requires config changes for prod rehearsal (TTL, scrape), call it out early so we can route through change approval. Neha/Sean asked that we don’t “sneak” monitoring changes day-of.

Victor Alvarez: noted. scrape interval change should be staging-only for now. for prod we can keep 60s but add freshness weighting + corroboration.

Allison Grant: :eyes: on prod burn rules too. I’ll propose gating paging w/ min volume + keep alerts as ticket-only during game day unless thresholds are exceeded.

Rafael Mendes: ok closing the loop. next sync tomorrow 10am. keep posting diffs/PR links here when you have them.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: We need to settle anti-flap + dedupe for perf-canary alerts before we turn on paging. Current incident-bot handler is basically “one Slack thread per firing alertname”, which is… not enough.

Allison Grant: +1. We’re going to get spam if p95 oscillates around threshold. Need: (1) alert rule dampening, (2) incident-bot dedupe key, (3) clear “resolved” behavior.

Aisha Bello: For alerting side: what are we thinking for minimum samples? If the canary is low QPS for overhead reasons, we can’t do 1-min windows.

Nadia Rahman: FWIW telemetry pipeline produces stable histograms at 1m resolution but variance is high under 50 req/min, especially for p95/p99. I’d strongly prefer the evaluator enforce a min sample size gate.

Sean Gallagher: From on-call perspective: fewer pages > faster pages. I’m ok delaying page by 10–15m if it reduces false positives.

Logan Wright: Dedupe key proposal: incident-bot should dedupe on a normalized “incident identity” that doesn’t include the volatile labels (baseline hash, run_id, pod). Something like:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric={latency_p95|tps_drop|queueing_p95},
  model,
  region,
  tier,
  severity
)
```

Logan Wright: optionally include hardware_sku but only if we *actually* route by SKU.

Allison Grant: Please don’t include runtime_build_id in dedupe key. That’ll explode threads during a rollout where we see both builds.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. But we still need the alert payload to show runtime_build_id + kernel tag for triage.

Caleb Johnson: +1 payload fields are fine. For dedupe, you want “same customer impact surface”. That’s model+region+tier+metric.

Aisha Bello: On anti-flap: can we do “X out of Y evaluations” gating? Example: p95 regression condition true for 3 out of last 5 minutes, AND min_samples >= N.

Nadia Rahman: Yes, evaluator already has per-window sample counts. Suggest min_samples per evaluation window AND across the combined dampening window.

Sean Gallagher: What numbers? If we set min_samples too high, we’ll never alert on lower-traffic regions.

Mateo Alvarez: For phase 1 (us-east), canary QPS should be enough. We can tune region-by-region later. But we need sane defaults now.

Allison Grant: Proposed default for warning:
- window: 10m rolling
- condition: regression >= threshold for 2 consecutive evals (each eval 5m)
- min_samples: >= 200 requests total over 10m for that model/region/tier
- hold-down: once fired, don’t re-fire for 30m unless severity escalates

Allison Grant: Paging:
- window: 15m rolling
- condition: 3 consecutive evals (5m each)
- min_samples: >= 400 requests total
- hold-down: 60m

Rafael Mendes: That hold-down is for *new* incidents right? We still want updates posted in the existing thread.

Caleb Johnson: incident-bot can do: if dedupe_key exists and status=firing, post “still firing” every 15m max (rate-limit updates). And on resolved, post a resolution note.

Logan Wright: We should also set a max update frequency per dedupe_key. E.g. no more than 1 message / 10m per incident, unless severity changes.

Aisha Bello: Severity change handling: warning -> page should post in same thread but also page the on-call channel. Dedupe key includes severity right now, which would create a new thread.

Allison Grant: Good catch. Dedupe should not include severity then. Severity should be a field that can change within the same incident.

Logan Wright: Ok updated:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric,
  model,
  region,
  tier
)

thread_action:
- if new dedupe_key: create thread
- if existing: update thread
- if severity escalates: @pager + add "ESCALATED" line, keep same thread
```

Sean Gallagher: That works for me. One thread = one “thing broken”. If it gets worse, same thread.

Nadia Rahman: On min_samples: define “request” consistently. A canary run can be streaming; do we count per request or per token?

Mateo Alvarez: For regression definition we’re using request-level histograms (latency per request) and aggregate tokens/sec. So min_samples should be request count for latency metrics. For tokens/sec, maybe min_samples is “total generated tokens” to reduce noise.

Aisha Bello: +1. tokens/sec can swing with small token totals.

Allison Grant: Suggest:
- latency metrics: min_requests >= 200 (warn), >= 400 (page)
- tokens/sec metric: min_tokens >= 200k (warn), >= 400k (page)

Nadia Rahman: 200k tokens in 10m might be high for some models if we’re limiting overhead.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s not block on perfect numbers. Can we set tokens/sec min gate as “min_requests >= 100 AND min_tokens >= 50k” for warn, then revisit?

Sean Gallagher: Works. And we can disable paging for tokens/sec initially, only warn.

Allison Grant: Yes: page only on p95 latency regressions for top models in phase 1. tokens/sec stays warn-only until we have a week of data.

Caleb Johnson: Dedupe edge case: region outage causes canary timeouts, we might fire both “latency_p95 regression” and “canary_error_rate high”. Those should be separate dedupe_keys, but same root cause.

Rafael Mendes: That’s fine. On-call can correlate. Also, canary_error_rate should route to SRE but not page runtime.

Allison Grant: Routing matrix reminder:
- perf regression (latency/tps): #eng-runtime + oncall-runtime when paging
- canary health (errors/timeouts): #eng-sre first

incident-bot: (system) Noted: update planned for dedupe_key to exclude severity; rate-limit updates; resolution message enabled. (reactions: :white_check_mark:)

Logan Wright: I’ll update incident-bot handler accordingly. Need final list of fields in payload + link templates (Grafana dashboard + runbook).

Aisha Bello: I’ll update alert rule drafts: add min sample gate, X-of-Y evaluations, hold-down, and warn-only for tokens/sec. Will post PR for review.

Sean Gallagher: Please include in the bot message a single-line summary:
“model=…, region=…, tier=…, metric=…, observed=…, baseline=…, delta=…, samples=…, runtime_build=…, kernel=…”
The first message should be enough to decide if it’s real.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add “next steps” line with runbook link and who to page if kernel tag changed.

Allison Grant: Cool. Action items:
- Logan: incident-bot dedupe + update throttling + severity escalation in same thread
- Aisha: alert policy updates (min samples, X-of-Y, warn/page split)
- Nadia: sanity-check sample gates vs expected canary QPS (us-east)

Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. Let’s keep it conservative. I’d rather miss one mild regression than page the world for noise.

Aisha Bello: Severity change handling: warning -> page should post in same thread but also page the on-call channel. Dedupe key includes severity right now, which would create a new thread.

Allison Grant: Good catch. Dedupe should not include severity then. Severity should be a field that can change within the same incident.

Logan Wright: Ok updated:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric,
  model,
  region,
  tier
)

thread_action:
- if new dedupe_key: create thread
- if existing: update thread
- if severity escalates: @pager + add "ESCALATED" line, keep same thread
```

Sean Gallagher: That works for me. One thread = one “thing broken”. If it gets worse, same thread.

Nadia Rahman: On min_samples: define “request” consistently. A canary run can be streaming; do we count per request or per token?

Mateo Alvarez: For regression definition we’re using request-level histograms (latency per request) and aggregate tokens/sec. So min_samples should be request count for latency metrics. For tokens/sec, maybe min_samples is “total generated tokens” to reduce noise.

Aisha Bello: +1. tokens/sec can swing with small token totals.

Allison Grant: Suggest:
- latency metrics: min_requests >= 200 (warn), >= 400 (page)
- tokens/sec metric: min_tokens >= 200k (warn), >= 400k (page)

Nadia Rahman: 200k tokens in 10m might be high for some models if we’re limiting overhead.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s not block on perfect numbers. Can we set tokens/sec min gate as “min_requests >= 100 AND min_tokens >= 50k” for warn, then revisit?

Sean Gallagher: Works. And we can disable paging for tokens/sec initially, only warn.

Allison Grant: Yes: page only on p95 latency regressions for top models in phase 1. tokens/sec stays warn-only until we have a week of data.

Caleb Johnson: Dedupe edge case: region outage causes canary timeouts, we might fire both “latency_p95 regression” and “canary_error_rate high”. Those should be separate dedupe_keys, but same root cause.

Rafael Mendes: That’s fine. On-call can correlate. Also, canary_error_rate should route to SRE but not page runtime.

Allison Grant: Routing matrix reminder:
- perf regression (latency/tps): #eng-runtime + oncall-runtime when paging
- canary health (errors/timeouts): #eng-sre first

incident-bot: (system) Noted: update planned for dedupe_key to exclude severity; rate-limit updates; resolution message enabled. (reactions: :white_check_mark:)

Logan Wright: I’ll update incident-bot handler accordingly. Need final list of fields in payload + link templates (Grafana dashboard + runbook).

Aisha Bello: I’ll update alert rule drafts: add min sample gate, X-of-Y evaluations, hold-down, and warn-only for tokens/sec. Will post PR for review.

Sean Gallagher: Please include in the bot message a single-line summary:
“model=…, region=…, tier=…, metric=…, observed=…, baseline=…, delta=…, samples=…, runtime_build=…, kernel=…”
The first message should be enough to decide if it’s real.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add “next steps” line with runbook link and who to page if kernel tag changed.

Allison Grant: Cool. Action items:
- Logan: incident-bot dedupe + update throttling + severity escalation in same thread
- Aisha: alert policy updates (min samples, X-of-Y, warn/page split)
- Nadia: sanity-check sample gates vs expected canary QPS (us-east)

Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. Let’s keep it conservative. I’d rather miss one mild regression than page the world for noise.
text:
Rafael Mendes: We need to settle anti-flap + dedupe for perf-canary alerts before we turn on paging. Current incident-bot handler is basically “one Slack thread per firing alertname”, which is… not enough.

Allison Grant: +1. We’re going to get spam if p95 oscillates around threshold. Need: (1) alert rule dampening, (2) incident-bot dedupe key, (3) clear “resolved” behavior.

Aisha Bello: For alerting side: what are we thinking for minimum samples? If the canary is low QPS for overhead reasons, we can’t do 1-min windows.

Nadia Rahman: FWIW telemetry pipeline produces stable histograms at 1m resolution but variance is high under 50 req/min, especially for p95/p99. I’d strongly prefer the evaluator enforce a min sample size gate.

Sean Gallagher: From on-call perspective: fewer pages > faster pages. I’m ok delaying page by 10–15m if it reduces false positives.

Logan Wright: Dedupe key proposal: incident-bot should dedupe on a normalized “incident identity” that doesn’t include the volatile labels (baseline hash, run_id, pod). Something like:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric={latency_p95|tps_drop|queueing_p95},
  model,
  region,
  tier,
  severity
)
```

Logan Wright: optionally include hardware_sku but only if we *actually* route by SKU.

Allison Grant: Please don’t include runtime_build_id in dedupe key. That’ll explode threads during a rollout where we see both builds.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. But we still need the alert payload to show runtime_build_id + kernel tag for triage.

Caleb Johnson: +1 payload fields are fine. For dedupe, you want “same customer impact surface”. That’s model+region+tier+metric.

Aisha Bello: On anti-flap: can we do “X out of Y evaluations” gating? Example: p95 regression condition true for 3 out of last 5 minutes, AND min_samples >= N.

Nadia Rahman: Yes, evaluator already has per-window sample counts. Suggest min_samples per evaluation window AND across the combined dampening window.

Sean Gallagher: What numbers? If we set min_samples too high, we’ll never alert on lower-traffic regions.

Mateo Alvarez: For phase 1 (us-east), canary QPS should be enough. We can tune region-by-region later. But we need sane defaults now.

Allison Grant: Proposed default for warning:
- window: 10m rolling
- condition: regression >= threshold for 2 consecutive evals (each eval 5m)
- min_samples: >= 200 requests total over 10m for that model/region/tier
- hold-down: once fired, don’t re-fire for 30m unless severity escalates

Allison Grant: Paging:
- window: 15m rolling
- condition: 3 consecutive evals (5m each)
- min_samples: >= 400 requests total
- hold-down: 60m

Rafael Mendes: That hold-down is for *new* incidents right? We still want updates posted in the existing thread.

Caleb Johnson: incident-bot can do: if dedupe_key exists and status=firing, post “still firing” every 15m max (rate-limit updates). And on resolved, post a resolution note.

source.content:
Aisha Bello: Putting up a strawman for alerting on the new tiered latency dashboards. Main open question: do we ever *page* on fast-tier p99? Or keep it ticket-only unless error budget burn triggers?

Aisha Bello: Proposed initial alerts (Hosted):
- fast tier: p99 end-to-end latency > SLO target * 1.25 for 10m (per region, aggregated over top models) -> ???
- standard tier: p95 > SLO target * 1.25 for 15m -> ticket
- cost-efficient: p95 > target * 1.35 for 20m -> ticket
- any tier: queue_depth p95 > X for 10m (route level) -> ticket
Also add burn-rate alerts on the tier SLOs for paging.

Sean Gallagher: thx. my take: page on burn rate, not raw p99. p99 is too noisy + we’ll end up paging on a handful of routes/models.

Allison Grant: +1. raw p99 alert will flap hard with low volume. also “per region aggregated over top models” is ambiguous; if we roll tier label, volume per series goes down.

Rafael Mendes: agreeing w/ Sean/Allison. we should page on *impact*, not just “bad number”. burn-rate tells us when we’re actually eating budget.

Aisha Bello: ok but the counterpoint: fast tier customers will yell immediately when p99 is janky even if burn rate stays under paging threshold (short spikes). do we want a fast-tier p99 page as a safety net during rollout?

Sean Gallagher: maybe only during canary + timeboxed, with aggressive suppression and a clean rollback knob.

Paula Mendes: if this is rollout-specific, can we treat it as a canary gate signal instead of oncall paging? i.e. automation blocks expansion / triggers rollback, but pages only if it persists after rollback fails.

Caleb Johnson: also remember we’re adding reason codes + tier labels. if we page on fast-tier p99 we need to include the “effective profile” + reason_code breakdown in the alert so oncall can tell if it’s override/overload-mode/etc.

Logan Wright: another concern: p99 per-tier per-model per-region is cardinality city. if alert queries accidentally fan out, we’ll melt metrics infra + also spam.

Aisha Bello: yeah. the dashboard spec uses top-N models by traffic and groups the rest into “other”. alert queries should mirror that.

Allison Grant: what’s the SLO doc saying for fast tier? is it p99 or p95 for streaming vs non-streaming? we probably need separate signals.

Aisha Bello: for streaming, we track TTFT p95 + inter-chunk gap p99 (ish). the p99 “end-to-end” is mostly for non-streaming.

Julian Moore: from runtime side, a lot of fast-tier pain shows up as queue wait. can we alert on queue_wait_ms p99 for fast-tier rather than total latency? it’s more actionable (scheduler/backpressure).

Sean Gallagher: queue wait is good for diagnosis but i’d still prefer paging based on customer impact. burn-rate on TTFT + E2E seems safer.

Nadia Rahman: for cost-efficient, latency might drift but still “acceptable”. the bigger risk is throughput collapse (batching misconfig) causing cost spike. do we want an alert on $/1K tokens delta by tier?

Allison Grant: cost alert yes, but not as a page initially. it’s a product-facing metric too; send to #optimize + ticket.

Rafael Mendes: circling back: we still need something during the canary window that catches fast-tier regressions quickly.

Aisha Bello: proposal v2:
1) Paging:
- Tier SLO burn-rate pages (multiwindow multi-burn):
  - fast tier: 2% budget burn in 1h AND 5% in 6h (or whatever standard template is)
  - standard tier: same but slightly less aggressive
- “rollout guardrail” page only while flag is enabled in a region:
  - fast tier p99 > target*1.35 for 15m AND RPS > min_threshold AND affects >=2 of top-5 models in that region
2) Ticketing:
- fast tier p99 > target*1.25 for 20m (same gating) -> ticket
- standard tier p95 > target*1.25 for 30m -> ticket
- cost-efficient tier p95 > target*1.35 for 45m -> ticket
- queue_wait_ms p95 > threshold -> ticket
Does this thread the needle?

Sean Gallagher: this is closer. the “flag enabled” gating is key. otherwise we’ll be living in that page forever.

Allison Grant: i like the >=2 top models condition. also add “exclude deploy windows” or at least annotate w/ runtime release train events.

Rafael Mendes: +1. for canary we also want explicit abort criteria. if fast-tier guardrail fires, automation should pause rollout and notify runtime + SRE, not just page.

Paula Mendes: i can hook the rollout automation to the same query + send a slack notify + flip the expansion off. but we need a single source of truth query (no drift between “automation” and “alert”).

Caleb Johnson: if we do this, alert payload needs to include:
- region
- tier
- top models affected + their p99
- queue_wait p99 vs compute p99
- reason_code distribution (tier_default vs override vs overload_mode)
so oncall can immediately decide rollback vs investigate.

Logan Wright: please also cap query fanout. do a recording rule or pre-aggregate (topN) so the alert is a single series. otherwise we’ll get the “multi-dim alert” explosion.

Aisha Bello: ack. i’ll work with Caleb to define the minimal labels. probably: {region, tier} only for paging alerts. model breakdown only in the alert annotations (via topk in query) or link to dashboard.

Allison Grant: question: do we page on *fast-tier TTFT* rather than end-to-end? streaming customers care more about TTFT; e2e includes generation length noise.

Sean Gallagher: yes. for fast tier, page on TTFT burn-rate + guardrail on TTFT p95/p99 (depending on doc). e2e p99 maybe ticket only.

Julian Moore: runtime can expose TTFT split into queue vs prefill. we already have that in the new metrics taxonomy draft.

Aisha Bello: ok updated:
- fast tier streaming: TTFT p95 guardrail during canary
- fast tier non-streaming: E2E p99 guardrail during canary
- paging otherwise only via burn-rate
- tickets for sustained threshold breaches
I’ll draft the exact thresholds based on the tier SLO doc + current baselines.

Rafael Mendes: please also add “min sample size” gating. if low traffic, we should suppress or ticket only.

Allison Grant: +1, use RPS > X or requests > N in the lookback.

Nadia Rahman: and add a note: tier label cardinality controls are in place. we should avoid alerting on per-route tiers for now.

Aisha Bello: sounds good. action items:
- Aisha: update alert spec with burn-rate + timeboxed fast-tier guardrail; include sample-size gating.
- Caleb/Logan: confirm query shape + cardinality-safe aggregation.
- Paula/Rafael: wire into rollout automation pause/abort.
- Sean/Allison: sanity check paging policy before we turn on canary.

PagerDuty (bot): Reminder: Any new paging alert requires an owner, runbook link, and severity classification before enablement.

Sean Gallagher: pls link runbook to the tiered batching rollout checklist + rollback steps. we should keep oncall steps short: verify dashboards -> check reason_code -> decide rollback/flag off -> escalate runtime.

Aisha Bello: will do. I’ll put the runbook link in the alert annotations + add a one-liner “kill switch: feature_flag tiered_batching_defaults=false for region”.

Allison Grant: ship it. we can tune thresholds after first canary week but let’s not start with noisy pages.

Rafael Mendes: +1. make sure the guardrail page auto-disables when the flag is off.

Aisha Bello: yep. will implement as `and on(region) feature_flag_enabled == 1` style gating. will post the final query here for review.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: need to lock acceptance criteria for failover controller dry-run this week. what *exactly* must it output so we can call ENG-2415 “done”? drop bullets here.

Miles Thornton: +1. baseline: dry-run should behave like prod decisioning but with zero writes. Output needs to be enough for on-call to say “yes execute” or “nope false positive”.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side: MUST be deterministic given the same signal snapshot + config (no nondeterministic ordering). also include the “decision trace” so we can reproduce.

Mina Haddad: pls include policy diff output. like “route chat/completions tier=hosted: us-east weight 100→0, eu-west 0→100” and any fallbacks invoked (variant/model) if applicable.

Benji Okafor: and scope. “applies_to”: tenant tier / allowlist / max blast radius gates that would be enforced if executed. dry-run should show what it *would* block too.

Allison Grant: observability asks:
- emit a metric for dry-run evaluations + outcome (no-op / recommend_failover / recommend_rollback)
- include evidence signals with current values + thresholds (reachability, 5xx burn, p99 burn, capacity headroom)
- timestamps + TTL on signal freshness. biggest pain is “is this stale?”

Sergio Costa: +1 on freshness. also: can we standardize output JSON for the CLI and for incident-bot to post? otherwise we’ll end up with 3 formats.

Devon Price: for incident-bot: minimum fields to post a summary:
- region
- recommended_action (failover/rollback/hold)
- confidence
- top_evidence (2–3 lines)
- dashboard links (region health, burn alerts, routing flips)

Rafael Mendes: what do we mean by “confidence”? we need something implementable, not hand-wavy.

Miles Thornton: propose a simple score 0–100 based on corroboration + signal priority. ex:
- reachability fail from perf-canary + control-plane probe = high
- only tail latency burn with no 5xx + normal reachability = low/medium
also include “reason_code” + “corroboration” list.

Jordan Lee: +1. keep confidence derived from explicit rule outcomes, not ML. e.g. `confidence_basis`: ["reachability_authoritative", "5xx_fastburn_confirmed"]

Benji Okafor: and dry-run must show anti-flap / hold-down implications:
- whether action is suppressed by hold-down timer
- time remaining
- last flip time

Mina Haddad: also show capacity gating outcome. we had staging false positives where us-east looked bad but eu-west had no headroom, so the “recommended action” should be “hold + shed” not “failover”.

Allison Grant: yes. output should include “target_region_viable”: true/false and why (headroom %, queue depth, GPU pool saturation, etc). even if headroom signal is coarse.

Rafael Mendes: do we need a “predicted impact” section? like estimated error reduction / latency change?

Miles Thornton: nice-to-have, not required for v1. but we should at least show the *intended* mitigations (failover vs load-shed vs circuit break adjustments).

Devon Price: can we require an “audit event” even for dry-run? not in the compliance sense, but so we can reconstruct decisions in incident timeline.

Jordan Lee: audit event ok as long as it’s clearly `mode=dry_run` and no policy write.

Sergio Costa: re format, suggest we standardize on one blob (JSON) and CLI just pretty-prints. incident-bot can render the same fields.

Rafael Mendes: ok summarizing acceptance criteria draft — pls react :thumbsup: / edits.

Rafael Mendes: **Dry-run mode acceptance criteria (v1)**
1) **No side effects**: does not write routing policy, does not trigger pool drain, no config changes. Only reads signals/config.
2) **Deterministic**: same signal snapshot + config => same output; includes `decision_trace_id` / trace for repro.
3) **Recommended action**: outputs `recommended_action` in {`FAILOVER`, `ROLLBACK`, `HOLD`, `SHED_ONLY`, `NOOP`}.
4) **Policy diff**: for FAILOVER/ROLLBACK/SHED_ONLY, outputs proposed policy changes as a human-readable diff + machine JSON (routes/regions/weights).
5) **Scope + safety gates**: includes tenant tier/allowlist context and whether any safety limits would block execution (max flips/hr, blast radius, approvals required).
6) **Confidence + reason codes**: outputs numeric `confidence` (0–100) + `reason_codes` + `confidence_basis` derived from rule/corroboration (no ML).
7) **Evidence signals**: outputs top contributing signals with current value, threshold, window, and **freshness** (signal timestamp + age/TTL).
8) **Anti-flap visibility**: indicates hold-down / dampening state and time remaining, plus last action timestamp.
9) **Target viability**: for failover recommendations, includes `target_region_viable` and key gating facts (capacity headroom, reachability to target).
10) **Telemetry**: emits metric + structured log/audit event with `mode=dry_run`, `recommended_action`, `region`, `reason_codes`, and links/ids for dashboard correlation.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: looks solid. only tweak: include `signal_snapshot_id` so we can replay in simulate endpoint later.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: +1 `signal_snapshot_id`. also ensure trace contains ordered list of evaluated predicates (so we can see which one “won”).

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: add explicit line: dry-run must report when it *would* require approval hook in prod (even though it can’t execute).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: on telemetry. for evidence signals, pls cap cardinality (don’t include tenant_id in labels). content in log is fine.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: +1 cap. also the diff should include “no-op” reason if it outputs HOLD/NOOP (eg hold-down active vs insufficient corroboration).

Devon Price: :thumbsup: works for incident-bot summary. i’ll align bot formatting to these fields once ENG-2415 lands.

Rafael Mendes: cool. I’ll paste this into ENG-2415 + link it from runbook. Miles/Jordan can you make sure `signal_snapshot_id` is feasible in the implementation? (even if it’s just a hash of inputs for now)

Miles Thornton: yep, we can do a stable hash of {signals+timestamps+config_version} as v1 and store full snapshot later.

Jordan Lee: works. if config_version is ambiguous, use policy bundle id (git SHA) so we can reproduce.

Rafael Mendes: done. thanks all.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: +1 cap. also the diff should include “no-op” reason if it outputs HOLD/NOOP (eg hold-down active vs insufficient corroboration).

Devon Price: :thumbsup: works for incident-bot summary. i’ll align bot formatting to these fields once ENG-2415 lands.

Rafael Mendes: cool. I’ll paste this into ENG-2415 + link it from runbook. Miles/Jordan can you make sure `signal_snapshot_id` is feasible in the implementation? (even if it’s just a hash of inputs for now)

text:
Rafael Mendes: need to lock acceptance criteria for failover controller dry-run this week. what *exactly* must it output so we can call ENG-2415 “done”? drop bullets here.

Miles Thornton: +1. baseline: dry-run should behave like prod decisioning but with zero writes. Output needs to be enough for on-call to say “yes execute” or “nope false positive”.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side: MUST be deterministic given the same signal snapshot + config (no nondeterministic ordering). also include the “decision trace” so we can reproduce.

Mina Haddad: pls include policy diff output. like “route chat/completions tier=hosted: us-east weight 100→0, eu-west 0→100” and any fallbacks invoked (variant/model) if applicable.

Benji Okafor: and scope. “applies_to”: tenant tier / allowlist / max blast radius gates that would be enforced if executed. dry-run should show what it *would* block too.

Allison Grant: observability asks:
- emit a metric for dry-run evaluations + outcome (no-op / recommend_failover / recommend_rollback)
- include evidence signals with current values + thresholds (reachability, 5xx burn, p99 burn, capacity headroom)
- timestamps + TTL on signal freshness. biggest pain is “is this stale?”

Sergio Costa: +1 on freshness. also: can we standardize output JSON for the CLI and for incident-bot to post? otherwise we’ll end up with 3 formats.

Devon Price: for incident-bot: minimum fields to post a summary:
- region
- recommended_action (failover/rollback/hold)
- confidence
- top_evidence (2–3 lines)
- dashboard links (region health, burn alerts, routing flips)

Rafael Mendes: what do we mean by “confidence”? we need something implementable, not hand-wavy.

Miles Thornton: propose a simple score 0–100 based on corroboration + signal priority. ex:
- reachability fail from perf-canary + control-plane probe = high
- only tail latency burn with no 5xx + normal reachability = low/medium
also include “reason_code” + “corroboration” list.

Jordan Lee: +1. keep confidence derived from explicit rule outcomes, not ML. e.g. `confidence_basis`: ["reachability_authoritative", "5xx_fastburn_confirmed"]

Benji Okafor: and dry-run must show anti-flap / hold-down implications:
- whether action is suppressed by hold-down timer
- time remaining
- last flip time

Mina Haddad: also show capacity gating outcome. we had staging false positives where us-east looked bad but eu-west had no headroom, so the “recommended action” should be “hold + shed” not “failover”.

Allison Grant: yes. output should include “target_region_viable”: true/false and why (headroom %, queue depth, GPU pool saturation, etc). even if headroom signal is coarse.

Rafael Mendes: do we need a “predicted impact” section? like estimated error reduction / latency change?

Miles Thornton: nice-to-have, not required for v1. but we should at least show the *intended* mitigations (failover vs load-shed vs circuit break adjustments).

Devon Price: can we require an “audit event” even for dry-run? not in the compliance sense, but so we can reconstruct decisions in incident timeline.

Jordan Lee: audit event ok as long as it’s clearly `mode=dry_run` and no policy write.

Sergio Costa: re format, suggest we standardize on one blob (JSON) and CLI just pretty-prints. incident-bot can render the same fields.

Rafael Mendes: ok summarizing acceptance criteria draft — pls react :thumbsup: / edits.

Rafael Mendes: **Dry-run mode acceptance criteria (v1)**
1) **No side effects**: does not write routing policy, does not trigger pool drain, no config changes. Only reads signals/config.
2) **Deterministic**: same signal snapshot + config => same output; includes `decision_trace_id` / trace for repro.
3) **Recommended action**: outputs `recommended_action` in {`FAILOVER`, `ROLLBACK`, `HOLD`, `SHED_ONLY`, `NOOP`}.
4) **Policy diff**: for FAILOVER/ROLLBACK/SHED_ONLY, outputs proposed policy changes as a human-readable diff + machine JSON (routes/regions/weights).
5) **Scope + safety gates**: includes tenant tier/allowlist context and whether any safety limits would block execution (max flips/hr, blast radius, approvals required).
6) **Confidence + reason codes**: outputs numeric `confidence` (0–100) + `reason_codes` + `confidence_basis` derived from rule/corroboration (no ML).
7) **Evidence signals**: outputs top contributing signals with current value, threshold, window, and **freshness** (signal timestamp + age/TTL).
8) **Anti-flap visibility**: indicates hold-down / dampening state and time remaining, plus last action timestamp.
9) **Target viability**: for failover recommendations, includes `target_region_viable` and key gating facts (capacity headroom, reachability to target).
10) **Telemetry**: emits metric + structured log/audit event with `mode=dry_run`, `recommended_action`, `region`, `reason_codes`, and links/ids for dashboard correlation.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: looks solid. only tweak: include `signal_snapshot_id` so we can replay in simulate endpoint later.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: +1 `signal_snapshot_id`. also ensure trace contains ordered list of evaluated predicates (so we can see which one “won”).

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: add explicit line: dry-run must report when it *would* require approval hook in prod (even though it can’t execute).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: on telemetry. for evidence signals, pls cap cardinality (don’t include tenant_id in labels). content in log is fine.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: +1 cap. also the diff should include “no-op” reason if it outputs HOLD/NOOP (eg hold-down active vs insufficient corroboration).

Devon Price: :thumbsup: works for incident-bot summary. i’ll align bot formatting to these fields once ENG-2415 lands.

Rafael Mendes: cool. I’ll paste this into ENG-2415 + link it from runbook. Miles/Jordan can you make sure `signal_snapshot_id` is feasible in the implementation? (even if it’s just a hash of inputs for now)

Miles Thornton: yep, we can do a stable hash of {signals+timestamps+config_version} as v1 and store full snapshot later.

Jordan Lee: works. if config_version is ambiguous, use policy bundle id (git SHA) so we can reproduce.

Rafael Mendes: done. thanks all.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: heading into prod game day next week — can we align on *must-have* dashboards + alert tuning? I don’t want us staring at 12 tabs and missing the one signal that matters. What’s the minimum set + what annotations do we need in place before go/no-go?

Allison Grant: +1. For game day we need a single “Failover Game Day” folder (Grafana) with panels in the order we’ll use them.
Must-have (IMO):
1) Region health aggregate (overall + per-signal)
2) Cross-region reachability probes (perf-canary) w/ p50/p95 + failure rate
3) API 5xx burn rate (multiwindow) split by region + route group
4) Tail latency burn rate (p99) split by region + route group
5) Capacity headroom + queue depth per region (hosted + dedicated)
6) Policy engine decisions: recommended vs executed flips, reason + confidence
7) Gateway retry/timeout counters (to catch thundering herd)
8) Dedicated drain progress if we do any pool drain steps

Sergio Costa: re: “single folder” — yes. I can pull together a dashboard with those panels, but we need to agree on the dimensions or it’s going to explode.
Also: do we want panels by `tier` (hosted free/pro/enterprise, dedicated) or keep it simpler for game day?

Aisha Bello: for *game day* I’d keep it to `tier in (hosted_prod, dedicated_prod)` and *no tenant labels*.
We can add a drilldown dashboard later. Otherwise cardinality + slower queries when everything’s already on fire.

Miles Thornton: + keep the “region health aggregate” panel as the *top* panel. That’s the entry point for “is this real impairment or just one service”.
Also want a panel showing “corroboration signals present” (reachability + 5xx + latency + capacity) since ADR-042 requires corroboration for automation.

Allison Grant: yep. We can model that as a table panel:
- signal
- current state (OK/WARN/FAIL)
- time since state change
- source (probe / SLO burn / capacity)

Victor Alvarez: networking asks: we need *some* view into DNS + anycast symptoms. Even if it’s just:
- resolver success rate (internal)
- upstream provider status ping (best effort)
- connection error codes to edge
In past events “reachability fail” was actually resolver weirdness.

Rafael Mendes: agree. But keep it lightweight. If it becomes a “network deep dive” it won’t get used.

Sergio Costa: ok, I’ll add a “Reachability + DNS” row:
- perf-canary probe success rate per region pair (us-east→eu-west etc)
- edge connect errors (SYN timeout / TLS handshake)
- dns resolution time + NXDOMAIN rate (internal resolver)

Devon Price: on annotations: we *need* automation-driven annotations or the timeline is going to be a mess.
Proposal:
- Every failover controller action emits an annotation event (recommend / execute / rollback)
- Manual operator actions via CLI also emit annotations (even in dry-run)
- Incident-bot posts a summary in-thread + link to dashboard with the time range pre-set

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please tie annotation names to runbook steps. If runbook says “Step 4: enable failover flag”, the annotation should literally say `RUNBOOK_STEP_4 enable flag` or similar.
(Otherwise people will write “did the thing” and post no details)

Allison Grant: Devon — do we already have audit events in the pipeline? If not, Grafana annotations won’t happen automatically.

Devon Price: we have the new failover audit event schema landing this week (from routing). I can wire a small exporter -> Loki annotations feed.
Worst case we use manual annotations (Grafana API) from incident-bot.

Aisha Bello: alert tuning: for game day specifically, we should:
- route pages to *game day oncall* only (no broad paging)
- set burn alerts to `notify` but not `page` unless we exceed rollback criteria
- add annotation links to “what does this alert mean” + the exact query

Miles Thornton: what are rollback criteria again for the drill? I want those *on* the dashboard.

Rafael Mendes: draft was:
- sustained 5xx > 2% for 3 min for top 3 routes *or*
- p99 latency > 2x baseline for 5 min *or*
- automation flaps more than 2 times in 15 min
+ anything that looks like customer-visible sustained errors beyond the blast radius.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a “Game Day Guardrails” text panel at top with those thresholds + links to the runbook section.

Vanessa Ortiz: capacity panels: I can provide the headroom metric but define “headroom” carefully.
We should chart:
- `gpu_tokens_per_s_capacity` vs `gpu_tokens_per_s_demand`
- queue depth / pending requests
- autoscaler saturation (desired vs current)
And include a big red indicator if we’re <15% headroom in the target failover region.

Allison Grant: for hosted, also add cache hit rate (prefix/KV) if available — during failover it can tank and cost/latency go sideways.
But ok if that’s a v2.

Victor Alvarez: note: reachability probes should show *directionality*. us-east→eu-west != eu-west→us-east depending on egress path.

Sergio Costa: yep. Panel will show both directions.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add “paging noise” view? During rehearsal we got spammed by unrelated alerts.
A panel listing firing alerts in the last 30m + whether they’re tagged `failover_signal=true`.

Aisha Bello: I can tag the alert rules and create a filtered view. Also we should temporarily silence non-failover pages during the drill window (with an explicit exception list).

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. Action items:
- @Sergio Costa build the Grafana folder/dashboard with agreed rows + guardrails text panel
- @Devon Price wire annotations from controller/audit events + manual CLI hook fallback
- @Aisha Bello tune burn alerts for game day routing + tags + “firing alerts” panel
- @Vanessa Ortiz publish capacity headroom queries + thresholds
- @Victor Alvarez provide minimal DNS/connectivity metrics + queries
Deadline: EOD Friday for go/no-go review.

Miles Thornton: please also add a “policy flips count” graph (per 5m) — anti-flap should be obvious visually.

Sergio Costa: yep.
Also question: do we want a separate dashboard for Dedicated drain progress? Or include a collapsible row?

Rafael Mendes: include a collapsible row for now. If we expand scope later we can split.

Allison Grant: +1. Keep the primary dashboard usable for Hosted-only too.

Devon Price: for incident-bot, I’ll include:
- current region health summary
- last 3 automation decisions with reasons
- links: dashboard (time range `now-1h`), region health logs, runbook

Aisha Bello: one more: can we add an explicit panel for “SLO classification: regional vs partial degradation”? We kept arguing about that in rehearsal.
Even if it’s a simple counter of `region_event=true` vs `service_event=true` based on the aggregator output.

Miles Thornton: yes please. That directly impacts whether automation is even allowed.

Sergio Costa: ok, adding.
I’ll post a draft link here once it’s up.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: thanks all. Let’s treat this as a go/no-go requirement — no dashboard, no prod test.

Sergio Costa: draft dashboard link (WIP): https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/failover-gameday/failover-game-day?orgId=1
Missing still: DNS panel (waiting on Victor), annotations (waiting on Devon).

Victor Alvarez: will drop queries in this thread in ~1h. Need to sanitize label set first.

Devon Price: annotations exporter is in progress. If I can’t land it today, I’ll at least give you a grafana-annotations API script we can run from incident-bot.

Tomáš Novák: once this is in place I’ll update the runbook with “which panels to look at in what order” + screenshots.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. Keep updates in-thread.
